paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Lam Wai Keung s.3
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.
paragraphs.text_private: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Lam Wai Keung s.4
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.
paragraphs.text_private: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.

propositions.label: Cruelty to animals — s.3 Cap. 169
propositions.label_zh: 虐待動物 — 第169章第3條
propositions.statement_en: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.
propositions.statement_zh: 根據《防止殘酷對待動物條例》（第169章）第3條，殘酷毆打、踢踹、虐待、折磨或對任何動物造成不必要痛苦屬刑事罪行。故意刺傷或傷害動物明確屬於此條文的規管範圍。最高刑罰為罰款港幣200,000元及監禁3年。

propositions.label: Abandonment and neglect — s.4 Cap. 169
propositions.label_zh: 遺棄及疏忽 — 第169章第4條
propositions.statement_en: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.
propositions.statement_zh: 第169章第4條規定，在可能導致動物遭受不必要痛苦的情況下遺棄動物屬刑事罪行。動物主人有積極責任確保其動物不受不必要的痛苦或困擾。

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Chan Siu Ming
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.
paragraphs.text_private: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.

propositions.label: Sentencing factors for animal cruelty
propositions.label_zh: 動物虐待罪行的量刑因素
propositions.statement_en: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.
propositions.statement_zh: 就第169章動物虐待罪行量刑時，法院衡量痛苦的嚴重程度及持續時間、行為是否故意，以及動物是否死亡。故意刺傷或殺死寵物通常會被判處即時監禁，量刑起點隨預謀程度及殘酷程度而增加。

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 315 [1]-[3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The offence of attempted burglary under section 11(1)(a) and (4) of the Theft Ordinance requires proof of an attempt to enter a building as a trespasser with the intent to steal therein.

propositions.label: Elements of attempted burglary
propositions.statement_en: The offence of attempted burglary under section 11(1)(a) and (4) of the Theft Ordinance requires proof of an attempt to enter a building as a trespasser with the intent to steal therein.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 218 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard baseline sentence for domestic burglary is three years' imprisonment, which inherently accounts for a degree of premeditation and planning.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 218 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The baseline sentence for burglary can be increased for professional criminals who execute meticulously planned burglaries involving high-value property.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 218 [16]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A sentencing discount may be granted when an offender's mental illness contributed to the offence, reflecting their diminished culpability due to an abnormal state of mind.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 218 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard sentence for unlawful remaining in Hong Kong is 15 months' imprisonment for a first offender who pleads guilty.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 218 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining in Hong Kong should generally be consecutive, but the totality principle may allow for partial concurrency.

propositions.label: Starting point for domestic burglary
propositions.statement_en: The standard baseline sentence for domestic burglary is three years' imprisonment, which inherently accounts for a degree of premeditation and planning.

propositions.label: Enhancement for professional burglary
propositions.statement_en: The baseline sentence for burglary can be increased for professional criminals who execute meticulously planned burglaries involving high-value property.

propositions.label: Sentencing discount for mental illness
propositions.statement_en: A sentencing discount may be granted when an offender's mental illness contributed to the offence, reflecting their diminished culpability due to an abnormal state of mind.

propositions.label: Tariff for unlawful remaining
propositions.statement_en: The standard sentence for unlawful remaining in Hong Kong is 15 months' imprisonment for a first offender who pleads guilty.

propositions.label: Consecutive sentencing for burglary and unlawful remaining
propositions.label_en: Consecutive sentencing for burglary and unlawful remaining
propositions.statement_en: Sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining in Hong Kong should generally be consecutive, but the totality principle may allow for partial concurrency.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the principle from Sim Ka Wing that the same sentencing approach applies to both domestic and non-domestic burglary.
The present judgment applies the principle from Sim Ka Wing that the same sentencing approach applies to both domestic and non-domestic burglary.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's ruling in CHIU Peng Richard regarding the availability of a sentencing discount for offenders with mental illness.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's ruling in CHIU Peng Richard regarding the availability of a sentencing discount for offenders with mental illness.
The judgment applies the established principle from Lau Pang that sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining should be consecutive.
The judgment applies the established principle from Lau Pang that sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining should be consecutive.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1588 [25]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard baseline sentence of 2½ years imprisonment for burglary of non-residential premises applies to cases with no aggravating or mitigating factors, and already incorporates an element of planning and premeditation.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1588 [25]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard sentencing starting-point for burglary can be reduced for opportunistic offenders, similar to a sneak thief who enters an open premises to steal available items.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1588 [25]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard sentencing starting-point for burglary can be increased for offenders who are professional criminals.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1588 [26]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When enhancing a sentence, the increase must be substantial to have a deterrent effect; for serious offences, it should generally not be less than six months.

propositions.label: Burglary sentencing starting-point
propositions.statement_en: The standard baseline sentence of 2½ years imprisonment for burglary of non-residential premises applies to cases with no aggravating or mitigating factors, and already incorporates an element of planning and premeditation.

propositions.label: Adjustment for opportunistic burglary
propositions.statement_en: The standard sentencing starting-point for burglary can be reduced for opportunistic offenders, similar to a sneak thief who enters an open premises to steal available items.

propositions.label: Adjustment for professional burglary
propositions.statement_en: The standard sentencing starting-point for burglary can be increased for offenders who are professional criminals.

propositions.label: Substantiality of sentence enhancement
propositions.statement_en: When enhancing a sentence, the increase must be substantial to have a deterrent effect; for serious offences, it should generally not be less than six months.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that sentence enhancements must be substantial and generally at least six months for serious offences.
The present judgment applies the principle from this case that sentence enhancements must be substantial and generally at least six months for serious offences.

paragraphs.label: [2018] HKCA 159 [6]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for possession of a dangerous drug, the court must consider the quantity and nature of the drug involved, as well as the offender's criminal history, to determine an appropriate starting point.

paragraphs.label: [2018] HKCA 159 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: While the court may acknowledge the difficulties faced by refugees or asylum seekers, such as lengthy status verification processes, holders of Form 8 (recognizance papers) must obey the law, and sentencing principles for such individuals must be applied.

propositions.label: Sentencing starting point for drug possession
propositions.label_en: Sentencing starting point for drug possession
propositions.statement_en: When sentencing for possession of a dangerous drug, the court must consider the quantity and nature of the drug involved, as well as the offender's criminal history, to determine an appropriate starting point.

propositions.label: Consideration of refugee status in sentencing
propositions.label_en: Consideration of refugee status in sentencing
propositions.statement_en: While the court may acknowledge the difficulties faced by refugees or asylum seekers, such as lengthy status verification processes, holders of Form 8 (recognizance papers) must obey the law, and sentencing principles for such individuals must be applied.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in Wan Sheung Sum for determining the starting point in drug possession cases.
The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in Wan Sheung Sum for determining the starting point in drug possession cases.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 315 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The standard baseline sentence for non-domestic burglary is 2.5 years' imprisonment.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 315 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The conventional 2.5-year starting point already accounts for a degree of premeditation and planning typical in such offenses.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 315 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The sentencing starting point can be increased for professional criminals who carry out meticulously planned burglaries involving high-value property.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 315 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The same sentencing principles regarding planning and aggravation apply to both domestic and non-domestic burglary cases.

propositions.label: Starting point for non-domestic burglary
propositions.statement_en: The standard baseline sentence for non-domestic burglary is 2.5 years' imprisonment.

propositions.label: Planning inherent in burglary starting point
propositions.label_en: Planning inherent in burglary starting point
propositions.statement_en: The conventional 2.5-year starting point already accounts for a degree of premeditation and planning typical in such offenses.

propositions.label: Aggravating factors for burglary sentence
propositions.statement_en: The sentencing starting point can be increased for professional criminals who carry out meticulously planned burglaries involving high-value property.

propositions.label: Uniform principle for domestic and non-domestic burglary
propositions.label_en: Uniform principle for domestic and non-domestic burglary
propositions.statement_en: The same sentencing principles regarding planning and aggravation apply to both domestic and non-domestic burglary cases.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the principle from Sim Ka Wing that the same sentencing considerations apply to domestic and non-domestic burglary.
The present judgment applies the principle from Sim Ka Wing that the same sentencing considerations apply to domestic and non-domestic burglary.

paragraphs.label: [2008] HKCU 932 [19]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The judgment refers to established case law (Cheng Wai Kai) that defines what constitutes aggravating features that can increase the severity of a sentence.

paragraphs.label: [2008] HKCU 932 [23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When multiple separate and distinct offences are committed repeatedly over a short period at different locations, the totality principle justifies adopting a higher overall starting point and imposing partly consecutive sentences rather than wholly concurrent ones.

paragraphs.label: [2008] HKCU 932 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant's personal gambling habit and the resulting economic pressure to obtain money quickly to repay gambling debts are not acceptable as mitigating factors in sentencing.

paragraphs.label: [2008] HKCU 932 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A guilty plea and a previously clear criminal record can be considered effective mitigating factors in sentencing.

propositions.label: Aggravating features for sentencing
propositions.statement_en: The judgment refers to established case law (Cheng Wai Kai) that defines what constitutes aggravating features that can increase the severity of a sentence.

propositions.label: Totality principle for consecutive sentences
propositions.statement_en: When multiple separate and distinct offences are committed repeatedly over a short period at different locations, the totality principle justifies adopting a higher overall starting point and imposing partly consecutive sentences rather than wholly concurrent ones.

propositions.label: Non-mitigating personal circumstances
propositions.statement_en: A defendant's personal gambling habit and the resulting economic pressure to obtain money quickly to repay gambling debts are not acceptable as mitigating factors in sentencing.

propositions.label: Effective mitigation factors
propositions.statement_en: A guilty plea and a previously clear criminal record can be considered effective mitigating factors in sentencing.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN SUNG WING [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The definition of 'manufacture' under the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance is broad and includes acts connected with making, adulterating, purifying, mixing, separating, or otherwise treating a dangerous drug.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN SUNG WING [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A new prosecutorial practice of charging 'manufacturing' for acts where an offender merely prepares drugs for personal consumption, which technically falls under the statutory definition, may be inappropriate and can affect the proper forum for trial.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN SUNG WING [5]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Cases involving the technical manufacture of drugs solely for personal consumption, as opposed to commercial or distribution purposes, may be more appropriately dealt with in the Magistrates' Court rather than the High Court.

propositions.label: Manufacturing definition under DDO
propositions.statement_en: The definition of 'manufacture' under the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance is broad and includes acts connected with making, adulterating, purifying, mixing, separating, or otherwise treating a dangerous drug.

propositions.label: Prosecutorial charging practice for personal use preparation
propositions.label_en: Prosecutorial charging practice for personal use preparation
propositions.statement_en: A new prosecutorial practice of charging 'manufacturing' for acts where an offender merely prepares drugs for personal consumption, which technically falls under the statutory definition, may be inappropriate and can affect the proper forum for trial.

propositions.label: Forum appropriateness for technical manufacturing
propositions.statement_en: Cases involving the technical manufacture of drugs solely for personal consumption, as opposed to commercial or distribution purposes, may be more appropriately dealt with in the Magistrates' Court rather than the High Court.

paragraphs.label: LO KWONG YIN v. HKSAR [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The correct test for applying the proviso to dismiss an appeal is whether the conviction was inevitable, not whether there was a departure from accepted norms amounting to a substantial injustice.

paragraphs.label: LO KWONG YIN v. HKSAR [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court is concerned only with counsel's legal submissions, not with their private written opinions or reasons provided to the Director of Legal Aid certifying prospects of success.

paragraphs.label: LO KWONG YIN v. HKSAR [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court of Appeal is entitled to take the view that a conviction was inevitable, and such a determination is not open to challenge merely because it might be argued to defy logical analysis.

propositions.label: Proviso test for appeal
propositions.statement_en: The correct test for applying the proviso to dismiss an appeal is whether the conviction was inevitable, not whether there was a departure from accepted norms amounting to a substantial injustice.

propositions.label: Role of counsel's opinion
propositions.statement_en: The Court is concerned only with counsel's legal submissions, not with their private written opinions or reasons provided to the Director of Legal Aid certifying prospects of success.

propositions.label: Court of Appeal's discretion on inevitability
propositions.label_en: Court of Appeal's discretion on inevitability
propositions.statement_en: The Court of Appeal is entitled to take the view that a conviction was inevitable, and such a determination is not open to challenge merely because it might be argued to defy logical analysis.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM SHING [6]-[7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A person can be convicted of murder under joint criminal enterprise without personally committing the actus reus, if they actively participated in an agreement with others to commit grievous bodily harm with knives, and their conduct constituted encouragement to other participants.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM SHING [8]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: At common law and under statute, a person is guilty as an accessory if they aid, abet, counsel or procure the commission of an offence by another person, and they are guilty of the same offence.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM SHING [17]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The guilt of an accessory depends on their actual state of mind - what they actually contemplated - not what they ought to have contemplated. They are liable only for offences within their contemplation, which may include several alternative offences.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM SHING [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Inaction when under a duty to act can constitute evidence of aiding and abetting, such as when a car owner fails to prevent a dangerous driver from using their vehicle excessively.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN KAM SHING [31]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Joint enterprise liability is not the same as aiding and abetting liability. In joint enterprise, all participants are principals, whereas an aider/abettor is a secondary party to the principal's crime, though they may be charged as a principal.

propositions.label: Joint enterprise liability basis
propositions.statement_en: A person can be convicted of murder under joint criminal enterprise without personally committing the actus reus, if they actively participated in an agreement with others to commit grievous bodily harm with knives, and their conduct constituted encouragement to other participants.

propositions.label: Accessorial liability statutory basis
propositions.statement_en: At common law and under statute, a person is guilty as an accessory if they aid, abet, counsel or procure the commission of an offence by another person, and they are guilty of the same offence.

propositions.label: Mens rea for aiding and abetting
propositions.label_en: Mens rea for aiding and abetting
propositions.statement_en: The guilt of an accessory depends on their actual state of mind - what they actually contemplated - not what they ought to have contemplated. They are liable only for offences within their contemplation, which may include several alternative offences.

propositions.label: Aiding by omission with duty
propositions.statement_en: Inaction when under a duty to act can constitute evidence of aiding and abetting, such as when a car owner fails to prevent a dangerous driver from using their vehicle excessively.

propositions.label: Joint enterprise vs. accessorial liability distinction
propositions.label_en: Joint enterprise vs. accessorial liability distinction
propositions.statement_en: Joint enterprise liability is not the same as aiding and abetting liability. In joint enterprise, all participants are principals, whereas an aider/abettor is a secondary party to the principal's crime, though they may be charged as a principal.

edges.explanation: The judgment discusses how Jogee abolished parasitic accessory liability and replaced it with traditional accessorial liability principles.
The judgment notes that Jogee held Chan Wing Siu had 'taken a wrong turn' in developing joint enterprise doctrine.
The judgment cites Bainbridge as an example of the mens rea requirement in counselling and procuring cases.
The judgment cites Bainbridge as an example of the mens rea requirement in counselling and procuring cases.
The judgment applies this case as authority that inaction when under a duty to act can constitute evidence of aiding and abetting.
The judgment applies this case as authority that inaction when under a duty to act can constitute evidence of aiding and abetting.
The judgment applies this case as an example where traditional accessorial liability principles could not be applied with confidence in joint enterprise situations.
The judgment applies this case as an example where traditional accessorial liability principles could not be applied with confidence in joint enterprise situations.
The judgment applies this case as authority that guilt can sometimes be established under either traditional accessorial principles or joint enterprise doctrine.
The judgment applies this case as authority that guilt can sometimes be established under either traditional accessorial principles or joint enterprise doctrine.

paragraphs.label: [2003] UKPC 10 [16]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offence of unlawful assembly, the defendant must be shown to have acted as part of an assembly with others, not merely engaged in disorderly conduct alone. There must be a sufficient nexus between the defendant's conduct and that of other participants to justify regarding them as acting together.

paragraphs.label: [2003] UKPC 10 [31], [39]-[40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An essential element of the offence of riot is that the participants had a common purpose and the intention to help each other, by force if necessary, against any person who might oppose them in the execution of that common purpose.

paragraphs.label: [2003] UKPC 10 [65]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For participatory offences like unlawful assembly and riot, the statutory language requiring a person to be 'assembled together' with others imports a requirement of physical presence at the scene to be held guilty as a principal offender. This statutory requirement cannot be displaced.

paragraphs.label: [2003] UKPC 10 [81]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Mere presence at the scene of an unlawful assembly or riot is not enough to constitute 'taking part' or aiding and abetting. There must be some intentional activity in furtherance of the unlawful assembly or riot.

paragraphs.label: [2003] UKPC 10 [81]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A person who encourages or promotes a riot, whether by words, signs, gestures, or by wearing the badge of the rioters, is to be considered a rioter and liable to be arrested for a breach of the peace.

propositions.label: Participatory nature of unlawful assembly
propositions.statement_en: For the offence of unlawful assembly, the defendant must be shown to have acted as part of an assembly with others, not merely engaged in disorderly conduct alone. There must be a sufficient nexus between the defendant's conduct and that of other participants to justify regarding them as acting together.

propositions.label: Common purpose and mutual assistance for riot
propositions.label_en: Common purpose and mutual assistance for riot
propositions.statement_en: An essential element of the offence of riot is that the participants had a common purpose and the intention to help each other, by force if necessary, against any person who might oppose them in the execution of that common purpose.

propositions.label: Presence requirement for principal offender
propositions.statement_en: For participatory offences like unlawful assembly and riot, the statutory language requiring a person to be 'assembled together' with others imports a requirement of physical presence at the scene to be held guilty as a principal offender. This statutory requirement cannot be displaced.

propositions.label: Mere presence insufficient for liability
propositions.statement_en: Mere presence at the scene of an unlawful assembly or riot is not enough to constitute 'taking part' or aiding and abetting. There must be some intentional activity in furtherance of the unlawful assembly or riot.

propositions.label: Encouragement as participation
propositions.statement_en: A person who encourages or promotes a riot, whether by words, signs, gestures, or by wearing the badge of the rioters, is to be considered a rioter and liable to be arrested for a breach of the peace.

edges.explanation: The judgment applies Lam JA's approach requiring a sufficient nexus between a defendant's conduct and other participants to regard them as acting together in an unlawful assembly.
The judgment applies Lam JA's approach requiring a sufficient nexus between a defendant's conduct and other participants to regard them as acting together in an unlawful assembly.
The judgment cites the Queensland Court of Appeal's holding that mere presence in an unlawful assembly or riot is generally insufficient to found liability at common law.
The judgment cites this case regarding the conditions that must be met for a jury to be satisfied of a defendant's participation in a riot.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN-SUM, IVAN [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Upon conviction for murder, the court is legally required to impose a mandatory life sentence and has no discretion to impose any other sentence.

propositions.label: Mandatory sentence for murder
propositions.statement_en: Upon conviction for murder, the court is legally required to impose a mandatory life sentence and has no discretion to impose any other sentence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 2833 [3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court should consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is not excessive, particularly where combining quantities from separate counts would not alter the sentencing starting point.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 2833 [3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Sentences for multiple offenses arising from the same incident or course of conduct may be ordered to run concurrently to reflect the overall criminality without imposing a crushing total sentence.

propositions.label: Totality principle application
propositions.statement_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court should consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is not excessive, particularly where combining quantities from separate counts would not alter the sentencing starting point.

propositions.label: Concurrent sentencing for related offenses
propositions.statement_en: Sentences for multiple offenses arising from the same incident or course of conduct may be ordered to run concurrently to reflect the overall criminality without imposing a crushing total sentence.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 516 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence to rebut the presumption that a child is incapable of criminal intent (doli incapax) must not consist solely of proof of the criminal act itself. The prosecution must provide independent evidence that the child knew the act was seriously wrong.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 516 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The test for rebutting the doli incapax presumption is whether the child appreciated the act was seriously wrong, not necessarily that it was morally wrong. Proof of moral wrongness is one way, but not the only way, to establish this appreciation.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 516 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The timing of an application to amend charges is critical; a late amendment increases the risk of causing injustice to the defence. The court must consider whether the amendment would prejudice the defence's ability to have a fair trial.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 516 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In the context of Hong Kong's compulsory education system, a court may reasonably infer that a child of school age possesses a basic understanding of the need to obey the law and the discretion to discern between good and evil, which is relevant to the doli incapax presumption.

propositions.label: Doli incapax presumption rebuttal evidence
propositions.statement_en: Evidence to rebut the presumption that a child is incapable of criminal intent (doli incapax) must not consist solely of proof of the criminal act itself. The prosecution must provide independent evidence that the child knew the act was seriously wrong.

propositions.label: Doli incapax test nature
propositions.statement_en: The test for rebutting the doli incapax presumption is whether the child appreciated the act was seriously wrong, not necessarily that it was morally wrong. Proof of moral wrongness is one way, but not the only way, to establish this appreciation.

propositions.label: Amendment of charges timing and prejudice
propositions.label_en: Amendment of charges timing and prejudice
propositions.statement_en: The timing of an application to amend charges is critical; a late amendment increases the risk of causing injustice to the defence. The court must consider whether the amendment would prejudice the defence's ability to have a fair trial.

propositions.label: Inference of child's knowledge from education
propositions.label_en: Inference of child's knowledge from education
propositions.statement_en: In the context of Hong Kong's compulsory education system, a court may reasonably infer that a child of school age possesses a basic understanding of the need to obey the law and the discretion to discern between good and evil, which is relevant to the doli incapax presumption.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence to rebut doli incapax cannot be mere proof of the criminal act.
The present judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence to rebut doli incapax cannot be mere proof of the criminal act.
The judgment cites Runeckles to support the distinction between an act being 'seriously wrong' and 'morally wrong' for the doli incapax test.

paragraphs.label: [1996] AC 1 [para 30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence to rebut the presumption that a child is incapable of criminal intent (doli incapax) must not consist solely of proof that the child committed the criminal act itself. The prosecution must provide independent evidence demonstrating the child's knowledge that the act was seriously wrong.

paragraphs.label: [1996] AC 1 [para 30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: To rebut the doli incapax presumption, the prosecution must prove the child knew the act was seriously wrong, not merely naughty or mischievous. This understanding of wrongfulness is not necessarily tied to moral wrongfulness, though proving moral wrongfulness can assist the prosecution's case.

paragraphs.label: [1996] AC 1 [para 53]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The later an application to amend charges is made, the more likely it is to cause injustice to the defence. The court must consider whether the amendment would prejudice the defence, particularly if made after the prosecution case has closed.

paragraphs.label: [1996] AC 1 [para 57]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where an improper amendment to charges necessitates a retrial, a judge in the subsequent proceedings has the power to stay that retrial and should exercise that power if the amendment caused injustice.

propositions.label: Doli incapax presumption rebuttal evidence
propositions.statement_en: Evidence to rebut the presumption that a child is incapable of criminal intent (doli incapax) must not consist solely of proof that the child committed the criminal act itself. The prosecution must provide independent evidence demonstrating the child's knowledge that the act was seriously wrong.

propositions.label: Nature of wrongfulness in doli incapax
propositions.label_en: Nature of wrongfulness in doli incapax
propositions.statement_en: To rebut the doli incapax presumption, the prosecution must prove the child knew the act was seriously wrong, not merely naughty or mischievous. This understanding of wrongfulness is not necessarily tied to moral wrongfulness, though proving moral wrongfulness can assist the prosecution's case.

propositions.label: Timing of charge amendments and prejudice
propositions.label_en: Timing of charge amendments and prejudice
propositions.statement_en: The later an application to amend charges is made, the more likely it is to cause injustice to the defence. The court must consider whether the amendment would prejudice the defence, particularly if made after the prosecution case has closed.

propositions.label: Power to stay retrial after improper amendment
propositions.label_en: Power to stay retrial after improper amendment
propositions.statement_en: Where an improper amendment to charges necessitates a retrial, a judge in the subsequent proceedings has the power to stay that retrial and should exercise that power if the amendment caused injustice.

edges.explanation: The judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence to rebut doli incapax cannot be mere proof of the criminal act itself.
The judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence to rebut doli incapax cannot be mere proof of the criminal act itself.
The judgment cites this case to support the distinction between an act being seriously wrong and merely morally wrong in the context of doli incapax.

paragraphs.label: [2013] NICA 23 [53]-[57]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An application to amend charges made late in the trial, particularly after the close of the prosecution case, may cause injustice to the defence and should not be allowed if it would necessitate a retrial covering the same grounds.

paragraphs.label: [2013] NICA 23 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: To rebut the presumption of doli incapax for a child defendant, the prosecution must prove the child knew the act was seriously wrong, not merely naughty. Proof cannot consist solely of evidence of the criminal act itself.

paragraphs.label: [2013] NICA 23 [24]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence to prove a child defendant's knowledge that their act was wrong must be independent and cannot be mere proof of the commission of the act charged.

paragraphs.label: [2013] NICA 23 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In the context of compulsory education, a reasonable inference may be drawn that a child of school age possesses the discretion to discern between good and evil, which is relevant to criminal liability.

propositions.label: Amendment of charges timing and prejudice
propositions.label_en: Amendment of charges timing and prejudice
propositions.statement_en: An application to amend charges made late in the trial, particularly after the close of the prosecution case, may cause injustice to the defence and should not be allowed if it would necessitate a retrial covering the same grounds.

propositions.label: Doli incapax rebuttal - knowledge of wrongfulness
propositions.label_en: Doli incapax rebuttal - knowledge of wrongfulness
propositions.statement_en: To rebut the presumption of doli incapax for a child defendant, the prosecution must prove the child knew the act was seriously wrong, not merely naughty. Proof cannot consist solely of evidence of the criminal act itself.

propositions.label: Doli incapax evidence requirement
propositions.statement_en: Evidence to prove a child defendant's knowledge that their act was wrong must be independent and cannot be mere proof of the commission of the act charged.

propositions.label: Inference of discretion from education
propositions.statement_en: In the context of compulsory education, a reasonable inference may be drawn that a child of school age possesses the discretion to discern between good and evil, which is relevant to criminal liability.

edges.explanation: The judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence of a child's knowledge of wrongfulness must be separate from proof of the criminal act.
The judgment applies the principle from YHN that evidence of a child's knowledge of wrongfulness must be separate from proof of the criminal act.
The judgment cites this case to illustrate that a child's knowledge an act is morally wrong is one type of knowledge that it is seriously wrong.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2425 [19], [28]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The right to hold a spontaneous demonstration may override the statutory requirement for prior notification in special circumstances, such as when an immediate response to a current event is warranted and delay would render the response obsolete. This exception applies only if the demonstration involves no illegal conduct by participants.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2425 [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The government has a positive duty to take reasonable and appropriate measures to facilitate the right of peaceful assembly, but this duty is not absolute and depends on all the circumstances of the particular case.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2425 [28]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The validity of restrictions imposed on the right of peaceful assembly must be examined by applying the principle of proportionality within the framework of the permitted restrictions listed in the relevant legal provisions.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2425 [33]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The police must be afforded a degree of discretion in taking operational decisions during assemblies, as they have access to information and intelligence not available to the public and are usually in the best position to make such complicated decisions.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2425 [42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The right of peaceful assembly does not extend to those who conduct themselves in a disorderly, intimidating, insulting, or provocative manner intended or likely to cause a reasonable fear of a breach of the peace, and protesters have no right to resort to violence.

propositions.statement_en: The government has a positive duty to take reasonable and appropriate measures to facilitate the right of peaceful assembly, but this duty is not absolute and depends on all the circumstances of the particular case.

propositions.label: Proportionality framework for assembly restrictions
propositions.statement_en: The validity of restrictions imposed on the right of peaceful assembly must be examined by applying the principle of proportionality within the framework of the permitted restrictions listed in the relevant legal provisions.

propositions.label: Police discretion in operational decisions
propositions.statement_en: The police must be afforded a degree of discretion in taking operational decisions during assemblies, as they have access to information and intelligence not available to the public and are usually in the best position to make such complicated decisions.

propositions.statement_en: The right of peaceful assembly does not extend to those who conduct themselves in a disorderly, intimidating, insulting, or provocative manner intended or likely to cause a reasonable fear of a breach of the peace, and protesters have no right to resort to violence.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 466 [20]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: WhatsApp messages can be admitted as evidence for a non-hearsay purpose, such as proving the act of communication itself or the state of mind of the communicator, rather than for the truth of their contents.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 466 [90]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant's claim that their inciting words were merely a joke can be rejected by the court based on an assessment of the context, content, and seriousness of the communication.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 466 [92]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Incitement to equip oneself with tools for criminal damage is particularly serious when directed at participants of an unauthorised assembly in a high-traffic public area frequented by vulnerable persons.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 466 [80]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Fresh evidence, such as post-sentence psychiatric reports, may be adduced on appeal to demonstrate a material change in the offender's circumstances that could affect the suitability of the original sentence.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 466 [105]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When a court determines that no other sentencing option is suitable in the circumstances, it must then consider imposing a term of imprisonment.

propositions.label: Imprisonment as last suitable option

paragraphs.label: [1974] AC 104 [19]-[28]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A spontaneous demonstration may be justified and override statutory prior notification requirements in special circumstances where an immediate response to a current event is warranted, provided the demonstration does not involve illegal conduct by participants.

paragraphs.label: [1974] AC 104 [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The government's positive duty to protect the right of peaceful assembly is not absolute; what constitutes reasonable and appropriate measures depends on all circumstances of the particular case.

paragraphs.label: [1974] AC 104 [33]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The police must be afforded a degree of discretion in taking operational decisions concerning public assemblies, as they have access to information and intelligence not available to the general public and face complex challenges.

paragraphs.label: [1974] AC 104 [33]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Interferences with the right of freedom of assembly are in principle justified for preventing disorder or crime and protecting the rights and freedoms of others, particularly when demonstrators engage in acts of violence.

paragraphs.label: [1974] AC 104 [42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The right of peaceful assembly does not extend to those who conduct themselves in a disorderly, intimidating, insulting or provocative manner intended or likely to cause a reasonable fear of breach of the peace.

propositions.label: Spontaneous assembly exception

propositions.label: Government's positive duty not absolute
propositions.statement_en: The government's positive duty to protect the right of peaceful assembly is not absolute; what constitutes reasonable and appropriate measures depends on all circumstances of the particular case.

propositions.label: Police discretion in operational decisions
propositions.statement_en: The police must be afforded a degree of discretion in taking operational decisions concerning public assemblies, as they have access to information and intelligence not available to the general public and face complex challenges.

propositions.label: Justification for interference with assembly
propositions.statement_en: Interferences with the right of freedom of assembly are in principle justified for preventing disorder or crime and protecting the rights and freedoms of others, particularly when demonstrators engage in acts of violence.

propositions.label: No right to disorderly assembly
propositions.statement_en: The right of peaceful assembly does not extend to those who conduct themselves in a disorderly, intimidating, insulting or provocative manner intended or likely to cause a reasonable fear of breach of the peace.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 275 [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offence of riot, the prosecution must prove that the alleged common purpose was shared, mutually understood, or communicated between the accused to the standard of a 'meeting of minds', rather than merely proving that the same purpose was held individually by each accused without mutual understanding.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCA 275 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A trial direction that enables a conviction without requiring the prosecution to prove all elements of the offences under sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance beyond reasonable doubt may render the trial unfair, not in accordance with law, breach the principle of legality, and violate the presumption of innocence.

propositions.label: Common purpose requirement for riot
propositions.statement_en: For the offence of riot, the prosecution must prove that the alleged common purpose was shared, mutually understood, or communicated between the accused to the standard of a 'meeting of minds', rather than merely proving that the same purpose was held individually by each accused without mutual understanding.

propositions.label: Fair trial and proof of elements
propositions.label_en: Fair trial and proof of elements
propositions.statement_en: A trial direction that enables a conviction without requiring the prosecution to prove all elements of the offences under sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance beyond reasonable doubt may render the trial unfair, not in accordance with law, breach the principle of legality, and violate the presumption of innocence.

paragraphs.label: [1982] QB 416 [9]-[12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The mental element (mens rea) for a statutory offence is determined through statutory interpretation, beginning with a presumption that mens rea is required, but this presumption can be expressly or impliedly displaced by the statute.

paragraphs.label: [1982] QB 416 [11]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When interpreting a statute to determine the mental element of an offence, the court must first decide if the presumption of mens rea is displaced. If it is displaced, the court must then determine what, if any, mental requirement replaces it.

paragraphs.label: [1982] QB 416 [53]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the 'likely limb' of the offence of taking part in an unlawful assembly (where the assembly's conduct is likely to cause reasonable apprehension of a breach of the peace), the test is objective. The subjective intentions of the defendants do not matter in assessing whether the likely consequence was present.

paragraphs.label: [1982] QB 416 [41]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If an offence, on its proper construction, falls within the fifth category established in Kulemesin, it dispenses with any mens rea requirement concerning a specified element (here, the likely consequence of causing apprehension of a breach of the peace).

paragraphs.label: [1982] QB 416 [45]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An offence found to be in the fifth Kulemesin category is not necessarily described as a 'strict or absolute liability' offence if it incorporates a significant degree of blameworthiness or culpable conduct in its definition.

propositions.label: Mens rea presumption as starting point
propositions.label_en: Mens rea presumption as starting point
propositions.statement_en: The mental element (mens rea) for a statutory offence is determined through statutory interpretation, beginning with a presumption that mens rea is required, but this presumption can be expressly or impliedly displaced by the statute.

propositions.label: Two-step construction for mens rea
propositions.statement_en: When interpreting a statute to determine the mental element of an offence, the court must first decide if the presumption of mens rea is displaced. If it is displaced, the court must then determine what, if any, mental requirement replaces it.

propositions.label: Objective test for 'likely' consequence
propositions.statement_en: For the 'likely limb' of the offence of taking part in an unlawful assembly (where the assembly's conduct is likely to cause reasonable apprehension of a breach of the peace), the test is objective. The subjective intentions of the defendants do not matter in assessing whether the likely consequence was present.

propositions.label: Fifth Kulemesin category dispenses with mens rea
propositions.label_en: Fifth Kulemesin category dispenses with mens rea
propositions.statement_en: If an offence, on its proper construction, falls within the fifth category established in Kulemesin, it dispenses with any mens rea requirement concerning a specified element (here, the likely consequence of causing apprehension of a breach of the peace).

propositions.label: Not all fifth category offences are 'strict liability'
propositions.label_en: Not all fifth category offences are 'strict liability'
propositions.statement_en: An offence found to be in the fifth Kulemesin category is not necessarily described as a 'strict or absolute liability' offence if it incorporates a significant degree of blameworthiness or culpable conduct in its definition.

paragraphs.label: [1976] RTR 251 [11]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The use or threat of force is not a necessary element for establishing the offence of incitement.

paragraphs.label: [1976] RTR 251 [15]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: There is no legal principle requiring the mens rea of the person being incited to match or mirror that of the inciter for the offence of incitement to be established.

paragraphs.label: [1976] RTR 251 [17]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The publication and circulation of material (like a newspaper article) can constitute an encouragement or endeavor to persuade to commit a crime, even if it is not addressed to any specific person.

paragraphs.label: [1976] RTR 251 [25]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When determining if material amounts to incitement, the court must look at the advertisement or publication as a whole, not in isolated parts.

paragraphs.label: [1976] RTR 251 [37]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For an offence under Article 24 of the National Security Law, the prosecution must prove the actus reus of organizing, planning, committing, participating in, or threatening specified activities, coupled with the element that these acts caused or were intended by the defendant to cause grave harm to society.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 712 [28]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The mere existence of a trustee-bankrupt relationship is not sufficient, by itself, to give rise to a common law duty of care owed by the trustee to the bankrupt.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 712 [36]-[38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where a statute (like the Bankruptcy Ordinance) creates specific duties for an officer and provides a correlative statutory remedy (e.g., under section 83), the proper construction of the statute indicates that the statutory remedy is intended to be the exclusive recourse, precluding a separate civil action for breach of statutory duty.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 712 [39]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The question of whether a common law duty of care arises depends on a broader analysis of the circumstances and is not determined solely by the construction of a relevant statute, which may only address claims for breach of statutory duty.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 712 [49]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where a detailed statutory regime demonstrates a policy to preclude civil claims for breach of statutory duties, that same policy may ordinarily operate to exclude the existence of a parallel common law duty of care in the same context.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFA 12 [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Leave to appeal may be granted on the basis of substantial and grave injustice if it is reasonably arguable that a lower court's exercise of discretion has miscarried.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 906 [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When expert evidence on foreign law is uncontradicted, the court should generally accept it and is not entitled to reject agreed evidence from both parties' experts based on its own research, unless the evidence is obviously false, obscure, extravagant, or patently absurd.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 906 [41]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant applying for a stay of proceedings on forum non conveniens grounds must establish both that Hong Kong is not the natural or appropriate forum and that another forum is clearly and distinctly more appropriate.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 906 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Even if a defendant establishes that another forum is more appropriate, a stay of proceedings may be refused if the plaintiff would likely be deprived of a legitimate juridical advantage by being forced to litigate in that other forum.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 906 [5]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A plaintiff may suffer a juridical disadvantage if a stay of proceedings would prevent its claim from being determined on the merits in the alternative forum, particularly where procedural rules in that forum restrict the ability to commence or advance substantive claims.

propositions.label: Expert evidence on foreign law

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 1119 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Before admitting expert opinion evidence, the court must first determine if the subject matter is outside ordinary experience and if the witness is qualified. Only then should it assess whether the factual premises of the opinion are themselves admissible.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 1119 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An expert witness may rely on a body of work by other specialists in the same field as an exception to the hearsay rule when forming their opinion.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 1119 [para before 14]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sexual offense cases where the prosecution must prove the defendant knew or was reckless about the complainant's lack of consent, the defendant's state of mind, intelligence, and ability to appreciate the situation are relevant considerations.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 1119 [para before 20]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Psychological validity tests assess whether a defendant answered test questions honestly (e.g., 'faking good' or 'faking bad'), not the veracity of their account of the alleged offense facts.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFI 1119 [21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An expert opinion regarding a defendant's particular susceptibility must be supported by identifiable traits in the defendant's personality profile, not merely by assertions the defendant made which the expert accepted.

paragraphs.label: [1975] QB 834 [26]-[27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Expert psychiatric evidence is only admissible to assist the court in evaluating whether a defendant's professed belief, for which there is some evidential foundation, could be symptomatic of a psychiatric illness that might negate mens rea.

paragraphs.label: [1975] QB 834 [26]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A psychiatrist's opinion that a defendant held a particular belief at the time of an offence cannot be based solely on the defendant's untested assertions made during an examination; there must be independent evidence that the defendant actually held that belief at the material time.

paragraphs.label: [1975] QB 834 [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Expert psychiatric evidence cannot be used to establish the factual existence of a defendant's state of mind (such as an innocent belief) where there is a complete absence of evidence supporting that state of mind at the relevant time.

propositions.label: Admissibility of expert psychiatric evidence
propositions.statement_en: Expert psychiatric evidence is only admissible to assist the court in evaluating whether a defendant's professed belief, for which there is some evidential foundation, could be symptomatic of a psychiatric illness that might negate mens rea.

propositions.label: Foundation for expert opinion on belief
propositions.label_en: Foundation for expert opinion on belief
propositions.statement_en: A psychiatrist's opinion that a defendant held a particular belief at the time of an offence cannot be based solely on the defendant's untested assertions made during an examination; there must be independent evidence that the defendant actually held that belief at the material time.

propositions.label: Limits of expert evidence on state of mind
propositions.label_en: Limits of expert evidence on state of mind
propositions.statement_en: Expert psychiatric evidence cannot be used to establish the factual existence of a defendant's state of mind (such as an innocent belief) where there is a complete absence of evidence supporting that state of mind at the relevant time.

paragraphs.label: [1973] AC 729 [para 3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence is considered relevant if it has logical value in proving or disproving a fact that needs to be established in the case. Relevance is not absolute but depends on the degree of probative value and the specific context of the proceedings.

paragraphs.label: [1973] AC 729 [3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Under Hong Kong law, an offence under the National Security Law is considered to have been committed within the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region if either the criminal act itself or its consequences occur within the Region.

propositions.label: Territorial application of NSL offences
propositions.statement_en: Under Hong Kong law, an offence under the National Security Law is considered to have been committed within the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region if either the criminal act itself or its consequences occur within the Region.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFA 18 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court of Final Appeal is not a sentencing court and should only hear appeals on sentencing principles in exceptional circumstances. The primary function of sentencing lies with the trial court, subject to review by the Court of Appeal.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFA 18 [8]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Appeals to the Court of Final Appeal concerning sentencing should be limited to matters of principle and should constitute only the rarest of cases.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFA 17 [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court is examining the legal test for establishing a common purpose in the offence of riot, specifically whether the prosecution must prove a mutual understanding or meeting of minds between the accused, or whether it is sufficient that each accused individually held the same purpose without proof of communication or mutual understanding.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCFA 17 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court is considering whether a judicial direction could allow a conviction without the prosecution proving all elements of the offences beyond reasonable doubt, potentially rendering the trial unfair, contrary to law, the principle of legality, and in breach of the presumption of innocence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 991 [28]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For indecent assault involving touching over clothing without force or coercion, a starting point of 3 years' imprisonment may be appropriate, even where the circumstances were frightening for the young victim.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 991 [52]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A guilty plea that spares the victim from giving evidence is generally recognized, but the standard one-third discount for a timely plea is considered the high watermark, with greater discounts reserved for exceptional cases.

propositions.label: Sentencing starting point for indecent assault without force
propositions.label_en: Sentencing starting point for indecent assault without force

propositions.label: Discount for guilty plea sparing victim testimony

propositions.statement_en: A guilty plea that spares the victim from giving evidence is generally recognized, but the standard one-third discount for a timely plea is considered the high watermark, with greater discounts reserved for exceptional cases.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1324 [17]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Incitement to participate in an unauthorized assembly is established when a person, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, urges or encourages the general public to knowingly take part in such an assembly, regardless of the eventual turnout.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1324 [38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A person holds or organizes an unauthorized assembly when they participate in leading, arranging, or publicly promoting a procession or gathering that lacks the required authorization.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1324 [100]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Incitement of the general public to join an unauthorized assembly is a serious offense where an immediate custodial sentence is the only appropriate sentencing option, particularly given the inherent risk of public disorder in a volatile atmosphere.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1324 [109]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When assessing culpability for holding or organizing an unauthorized assembly, the court must consider the organizer's intention for the procession to continue, especially where it is shown they would not have stopped voluntarily.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1324 [65]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant's role in a joint offense of incitement or organizing an unauthorized assembly is assessed based on their active participation, such as speaking, holding banners, or leading the crowd, not merely on passive presence.

propositions.label: Incitement to unauthorized assembly
propositions.statement_en: Incitement to participate in an unauthorized assembly is established when a person, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, urges or encourages the general public to knowingly take part in such an assembly, regardless of the eventual turnout.

propositions.label: Holding or organizing unauthorized assembly
propositions.statement_en: A person holds or organizes an unauthorized assembly when they participate in leading, arranging, or publicly promoting a procession or gathering that lacks the required authorization.

propositions.label: Sentencing for incitement - custodial threshold
propositions.statement_en: Incitement of the general public to join an unauthorized assembly is a serious offense where an immediate custodial sentence is the only appropriate sentencing option, particularly given the inherent risk of public disorder in a volatile atmosphere.

propositions.label: Culpability for organizing - intention to continue
propositions.label_en: Culpability for organizing - intention to continue
propositions.statement_en: When assessing culpability for holding or organizing an unauthorized assembly, the court must consider the organizer's intention for the procession to continue, especially where it is shown they would not have stopped voluntarily.

propositions.label: Role assessment in joint incitement/organizing
propositions.statement_en: A defendant's role in a joint offense of incitement or organizing an unauthorized assembly is assessed based on their active participation, such as speaking, holding banners, or leading the crowd, not merely on passive presence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 645 [27]-[30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The sentencing approach for unlawful assemblies must consider punishment and deterrence to maintain public order, and is not limited to cases involving actual violence; the appropriate sentence depends on the specific circumstances of each case.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 645 [44]-[45]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Organisers of an unauthorised assembly can be held accountable for the consequences of the event, including unlawful acts that occur, and cannot avoid liability merely by declaring their intention for the event to be peaceful or by claiming the event concluded at a specific point.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 645 [44]-[45], [50]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When determining culpability for public order offences, the court considers whether the defendants were aware of and ignored a real risk of violence or disorder, particularly where intelligence of such risks was publicly available and recent history showed a pattern of escalation.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 645 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Repeated public assertions by defendants that their rights have been suppressed or that the law is unfair do not, by mere repetition, establish the truth of those claims or operate as a mitigating factor in sentencing.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 645 [66], [91]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: All individuals who are part of the core organising group of an unauthorised assembly can be found equally culpable, irrespective of the specific role each played (e.g., speaking to press, holding banners, chanting), as long as they were involved in the organisation.

propositions.label: Sentencing for unlawful assemblies
propositions.statement_en: The sentencing approach for unlawful assemblies must consider punishment and deterrence to maintain public order, and is not limited to cases involving actual violence; the appropriate sentence depends on the specific circumstances of each case.

propositions.label: Organiser liability for unauthorised assembly
propositions.statement_en: Organisers of an unauthorised assembly can be held accountable for the consequences of the event, including unlawful acts that occur, and cannot avoid liability merely by declaring their intention for the event to be peaceful or by claiming the event concluded at a specific point.

propositions.label: Assessment of risk in public order offences
propositions.label_en: Assessment of risk in public order offences
propositions.statement_en: When determining culpability for public order offences, the court considers whether the defendants were aware of and ignored a real risk of violence or disorder, particularly where intelligence of such risks was publicly available and recent history showed a pattern of escalation.

propositions.label: Mitigation by repetition of claims
propositions.statement_en: Repeated public assertions by defendants that their rights have been suppressed or that the law is unfair do not, by mere repetition, establish the truth of those claims or operate as a mitigating factor in sentencing.

propositions.label: Culpability of organisers
propositions.statement_en: All individuals who are part of the core organising group of an unauthorised assembly can be found equally culpable, irrespective of the specific role each played (e.g., speaking to press, holding banners, chanting), as long as they were involved in the organisation.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles from Wong Chi Fung, confirming that deterrent sentences for unlawful assemblies are not confined to cases involving actual violence.
The present judgment applies the sentencing principles from Wong Chi Fung, confirming that deterrent sentences for unlawful assemblies are not confined to cases involving actual violence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1100 [100]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Organisers of an unauthorised public procession can be held accountable for unlawful acts, violence, and damage occurring during and after the procession, even if they declared it over, provided the procession had a continuing presence and the acts are attributable to it.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1100 [71]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For public order offences, a deterrent sentence can be imposed even on offenders with clear records, as such offences constitute an exception to the general principle against deterrent sentences for first-time offenders.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1100 [74]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The use or threat of violence is an aggravating factor in sentencing for unlawful assembly offences, and the sentence must serve the dual purposes of punishment and deterrence to maintain public order.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1100 [106]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for public order offences committed as civil disobedience, the defendant's genuine beliefs and conscientious objections carry limited mitigating weight when balanced against the offence facts, consequences, and the need for deterrence, especially if the disobedience is not directly targeted at the specific law breached.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1100 [112]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Participants who jointly lead and organise an unauthorised procession can be found equally culpable for sentencing purposes, even if their individual roles (e.g., chanting, wearing masks) within the leading group differed.

propositions.label: Organiser liability for procession consequences

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1160 [66], [70], [75], [80]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A starting point of 12 months' imprisonment is appropriate for the offense of inciting others to knowingly take part in an unauthorized assembly, with adjustments made based on the defendant's role and personal circumstances.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1160 [67], [71], [76], [80]

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1160 [66], [70], [75]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A more active role in the offense and being on court bail at the time of the offense are aggravating factors that warrant an increase in the custodial sentence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1160 [60]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In the context of the prevailing circumstances at the time, an immediate custodial sentence is the appropriate starting point for offenses related to unauthorized assemblies, even where the gathering was peaceful and no tangible harm resulted.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 1160 [78]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant's previously clear record, passive role in the offense, and positive contributions to public service can be considered as mitigating factors in sentencing.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 457 [38]

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 457 [44]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for unlawful assemblies involving violence, the primary considerations are punishment of the offender and deterrence of others; the offender's personal circumstances carry little mitigating weight.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 457 [50]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Sentencing principles established for unlawful assemblies, such as public protection, penalty imposition, open condemnation, and deterrence, may be considered in sentencing for unauthorized assemblies, but the distinction between the two offences must be borne in mind.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 457 [51]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A sentence must be proportionate to the seriousness of the offence and the offender's culpability, and should reflect society's disapproval of the criminal conduct.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 457 [59]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A conscious decision to break the law and challenge public order during a period of social unrest and volatility constitutes a serious aggravating factor in sentencing.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 3089 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: To determine what constitutes a final judgment under section 31 of the Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal Ordinance, the court should apply the 'application approach' established in Shell Hong Kong Ltd v Yeung Wai Man Kiu Yip Co Ltd. Under this approach, a decision may be considered final even if it does not completely resolve the entire legal action, depending on the purpose and substance of the application, the specific issue determined, and the effect of that determination.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 3089 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A decision is final and not subject to being reopened if it addresses a pure question of law that arises before any assessment of risks related to granting or refusing bail, rather than being an appeal against the actual grant or refusal of bail based on risk assessment.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 3089 [18]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Bail pending appeal should only be granted in exceptional circumstances, and the decision rests clearly within the court's discretion.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 569 [30]-[34], [41]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When interpreting a statutory offence, there is a presumption that the prosecution must prove the defendant's mental state for each element of the offence, unless this presumption is clearly displaced by the legislature. Displacement requires consideration of factors including the offence's nature, seriousness, public protection needs, and practicalities of enforcement.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 569 [46]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offence of loitering causing concern for safety or well-being, the concern must be reasonable, which involves both a subjective test (whether the person was actually concerned) and an objective test (whether that concern was reasonable in the circumstances).

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 569 [49]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For certain offences where the consequence of the defendant's act is an element, the prosecution may only need to prove that the defendant's act caused the consequence, not that the defendant intended or had mens rea regarding that specific consequence.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 569 [69], [75]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A prosecution can be validly based on the doctrine of joint criminal enterprise even if not explicitly stated in the opening or summary of facts, provided the defence was aware of this basis and had the opportunity to address it during the trial.

paragraphs.label: [1986] AC 887 [30]-[34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When interpreting a statutory offence, there is a presumption that mens rea (a guilty mind) is required for each element of the offence, unless this presumption is clearly displaced by the legislature. paragraphs.label: [1986] AC 887 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: To determine if the presumption of mens rea is displaced, the court must consider: the nature and subject matter of the offence, its seriousness and penalty, social stigma, the need for public protection, practicalities of prosecution, and whether the defendant had sole access to the relevant information.

paragraphs.label: [1986] AC 887 [46]

propositions.label_en: Factors for displacing mens rea presumption
propositions.statement_en: To determine if the presumption of mens rea is displaced, the court must consider: the nature and subject-matter of the offence, its seriousness and penalty, social stigma, the need for public protection, practicalities of prosecution, and whether the defendant had sole access to the relevant information.

propositions.label: Objective test for 'reasonable concern'
propositions.statement_en: For an offence involving causing another person 'reasonably to be concerned', the concern must be reasonable, which involves both subjective and objective tests; it is not enough that someone subjectively feels concern.

propositions.label: Certainty and proportionality in offence definition
propositions.label_en: Certainty and proportionality in offence definition
propositions.statement_en: Legal provisions must be sufficiently specific and certain to enable the public to regulate their conduct and to avoid being overly broad or disproportionate, which would violate principles of legal certainty and proportionality.

propositions.label: Awareness of prosecution basis at trial
propositions.label_en: Awareness of prosecution basis at trial
propositions.statement_en: If the defence counsel made submissions at trial addressing a particular legal basis (like joint enterprise), it cannot later be argued that the defence was deprived of the chance to adduce evidence because they failed to grasp the prosecution's basis.

edges.explanation: The judgment criticizes following this case's definition of 'well-being' as too broad and potentially contrary to legal certainty and constitutional protections.
The judgment criticizes following this case's definition of 'well-being' as too broad and potentially contrary to legal certainty and constitutional protections.

The judgment notes criticism of applying this English civil case's definition of 'well-being' to a Hong Kong criminal case, as it leads to an overly broad interpretation.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2358 [30]-[34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When interpreting a statutory offence, there is a presumption that mens rea (a guilty mind) is required for each element of the offence, unless legislative intent clearly displaces this presumption.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2358 [46]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For an offence under section 160(3) of the Crimes Ordinance, the concern caused by loitering must be reasonable, which involves both subjective and objective tests, not merely the subjective feelings of the complainant.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2358 [49]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For some statutory offences where a consequence is an element, the prosecution may only need to prove causation of that consequence, not the defendant's mental state regarding that specific consequence.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2358 (4), [57]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The term 'well-being' in section 160(3) should not be defined so broadly as to equate to a general state of happiness or contentment, as this may lack the specificity and certainty required by law and infringe constitutional protections.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKCFI 2358 [75]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where the prosecution's case is fundamentally based on the doctrine of joint criminal enterprise, and the defence has addressed this in closing submissions, it cannot later be argued that the defence was deprived of a fair chance to respond.
