paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 923 [8]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The general rule is that the successful party is entitled to its costs, unless there are good reasons to depart from this norm.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 923 [9]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Good reasons for departing from the normal costs rule can include the grounds of appeal advanced and the conduct of the parties and their legal representatives during the appeal hearing.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 13 [2]-[3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An instrument is considered 'false' for forgery offences only if it tells a lie about its own origins or creation, such as who made it or the circumstances of its making, not merely if it contains false factual statements.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 13 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant who engages in conduct that brings suspicion and prosecution upon themselves, and whose actions are found to be reprehensible and intended to induce others to act on false documents, is not entitled to an award of trial costs.

propositions.label: Automendacity principle for forgery
propositions.statement_en: An instrument is considered 'false' for forgery offences only if it tells a lie about its own origins or creation, such as who made it or the circumstances of its making, not merely if it contains false factual statements.

propositions.label: Costs denial for self-induced prosecution
propositions.statement_en: A defendant who engages in conduct that brings suspicion and prosecution upon themselves, and whose actions are found to be reprehensible and intended to induce others to act on false documents, is not entitled to an award of trial costs.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 7 [38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A document is a false instrument under section 69(a)(vii) of the Crimes Ordinance if it is made in circumstances where it purports to be made in a certain way, or by a certain person, or with certain authority, but was not in fact made in that way, by that person, or with that authority.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 7 [39]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The traditional principle in forgery law, known as the automendacity principle, requires that for a document to be false, it must 'tell a lie about itself'—meaning the falsity must relate to the document's own making, authorship, or authority, not merely to extraneous facts or the underlying transaction it records.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 7 [29]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A document may be considered a false instrument if it purports to effect a genuine transaction but is in fact a sham—that is, the underlying transaction it records did not and was never intended to take effect as presented, rendering the document's representation of that transaction false.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFA 7 [22]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The offence of using a false instrument requires proof that the defendant used a document they knew was false, with the intention of inducing someone to accept it as genuine, and by so accepting it, to act to their own or another's prejudice.

propositions.label: Definition of false instrument under s.69(a)(vii)
propositions.label_en: Definition of false instrument under s.69(a)(vii)
propositions.statement_en: A document is a false instrument under section 69(a)(vii) of the Crimes Ordinance if it is made in circumstances where it purports to be made in a certain way, or by a certain person, or with certain authority, but was not in fact made in that way, by that person, or with that authority.

propositions.label: Automendacity principle in forgery
propositions.statement_en: The traditional principle in forgery law, known as the automendacity principle, requires that for a document to be false, it must 'tell a lie about itself'—meaning the falsity must relate to the document's own making, authorship, or authority, not merely to extraneous facts or the underlying transaction it records.

propositions.label: Sham transaction and instrument falsity
propositions.statement_en: A document may be considered a false instrument if it purports to effect a genuine transaction but is in fact a sham—that is, the underlying transaction it records did not and was never intended to take effect as presented, rendering the document's representation of that transaction false.

propositions.label: Use of false instrument with intent to induce
propositions.label_en: Use of false instrument with intent to induce
propositions.statement_en: The offence of using a false instrument requires proof that the defendant used a document they knew was false, with the intention of inducing someone to accept it as genuine, and by so accepting it, to act to their own or another's prejudice.

edges.explanation: The judgment notes that Donnelly was held to be binding and followed in later English cases, representing a wide interpretation of the forgery provision.
The English Court of Appeal in Jeraj followed Donnelly, applying the wide construction that a document can be false due to circumstances extraneous to its making.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court may reformulate a ground of appeal as a question of law of great and general importance to grant leave to appeal.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 7 [47]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For an offence involving a false instrument, the prosecution must prove that the accused knew the instrument was false at the time of the offence. The court is entitled to make such a finding based on the evidence.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 7 [44]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: On appeal, criticisms of the trial judge's reasoning on matters that are collateral to the core factual findings necessary for the conviction will not be addressed if those findings remain unaffected.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 7 [23]-[29]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An instrument may be found to be false based on its substantive content and context, not merely because a specific monetary consideration stated within it was not paid in the exact manner described, if the overall representation is untrue.

propositions.label: Knowledge of false instrument

paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court retains the power to consider substituting an alternative verdict if it finds the convictions for the primary offences cannot be sustained.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 3584 [31]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The fundamental legal principle is that every person's body is inviolate, and any physical touching of another person, no matter how slight, can constitute the offense of battery if done without lawful justification.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 3584 [31]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Physical contact that is reasonable in the circumstances, such as a light touch to gain someone's attention, generally does not amount to unlawful force or violence for the purpose of establishing assault or battery.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 3584 [36]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For an assault to be established on the basis of recklessness, it must be shown that the defendant acted without consideration as to whether the victim would apprehend immediate and unlawful violence.

propositions.label: Battery definition and bodily inviolability
propositions.statement_en: The fundamental legal principle is that every person's body is inviolate, and any physical touching of another person, no matter how slight, can constitute the offense of battery if done without lawful justification.

propositions.label: Reasonable contact exception
propositions.statement_en: Physical contact that is reasonable in the circumstances, such as a light touch to gain someone's attention, generally does not amount to unlawful force or violence for the purpose of establishing assault or battery.

propositions.label: Mental element for reckless assault
propositions.statement_en: For an assault to be established on the basis of recklessness, it must be shown that the defendant acted without consideration as to whether the victim would apprehend immediate and unlawful violence.

edges.explanation: The judgment references Collins v Wilcock for the principle that reasonable physical contact may not be unlawful force, but reaffirms the fundamental inviolability of the person.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A timely guilty plea warrants a substantial reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court has discretion to order sentences for separate offenses to run either consecutively or concurrently, based on the need to reflect the overall criminal conduct.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 216 [66]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In cases involving a continuing course of conduct or a sequence of events, it is not necessary for the defendant's guilty mind (mens rea) to be present at the very beginning of the unlawful act (actus reus). It is sufficient if the defendant formed the requisite intent at some point during the ongoing transaction or sequence of events.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 216 [58], [89]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When determining whether a defendant's conduct formed part of a sequence of events leading to a death, the prosecution does not need to prove the defendant intended there to be a sequence. The jury, applying common sense, must decide as a matter of fact whether the proven conduct was part of such a sequence. A continuous, daily occurrence is not required.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 216 [83], [85]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A person with charge, custody, or care of a child can be found guilty of cruelty not only through direct acts but also through acquiescence or connivance. This can be established by evidence that the person turned a blind eye to the child's obvious suffering and failed to intervene.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 216 [85]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for offences of cruelty to a child, the court is entitled to take into account the fact that the cruelty resulted in the child's death as a relevant and significant consequence when assessing the appropriate penalty.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 216 [94]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence of a defendant's misconduct towards one victim (X) must not be used by the jury to conclude that the defendant is more likely to have committed a similar offence against another victim (Z). No adverse inference should be drawn from such evidence in relation to the charges concerning the second victim.

paragraphs.label: [2011] ONCA 194 [66]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In cases involving a continuing series of acts (a single transaction), it is not necessary for the mens rea (intention to cause grievous bodily harm) to be present at the very beginning of the actus reus. It is sufficient if the defendant formed the requisite intent at some point during the ongoing sequence of events.

paragraphs.label: [2011] ONCA 194 [89]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A judge is not required to provide a legal definition of a 'sequence of events' to the jury. It is correct to direct the jury to use their common sense to determine, as a matter of fact, whether the proven acts formed part of a sequence of events that resulted in the victim's death.

paragraphs.label: [2011] ONCA 194 [89]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For a murder conviction where acts form a sequence, the jury must first be sure the defendant's conduct was part of a sequence of events leading to death. If so, they must then be sure that at some point during that conduct which caused death, the defendant intended to cause grievous bodily harm.

paragraphs.label: [2011] ONCA 194 [94]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A specific direction to the jury on how to handle potential disagreement (a Kevin Brown direction) is not required merely because there were multiple acts in a sequence. It is only necessary if there is evidence of actual jury disagreement on an essential ingredient of the offence.

paragraphs.label: [2011] ONCA 194 [94]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence of a defendant's misconduct towards one victim (X) must not be used by the jury to infer that the defendant is more likely to have committed similar acts against another victim (Z). No adverse inference should be drawn from such evidence in relation to charges concerning the other victim.

propositions.label: No requirement for Kevin Brown direction on jury disagreement

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 159 [45]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offense of arson, recklessness is established when the defendant, an adult of normal intelligence, is aware of a risk of property damage and, in the circumstances known to them, it is unreasonable to take that risk.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 159 [46]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A conviction requires the prosecution to prove every element of the charge beyond a reasonable doubt.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 159 [37]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may reject a defendant's testimony in its entirety if it finds the evidence to be unreliable and incredible.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 159 [36]

propositions.label: Mental capacity for criminal responsibility

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2234 [22]-[23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When a party knowingly commits further breaches of a court order after a prior ruling has clarified the order's requirements, those subsequent breaches must be treated more seriously as they demonstrate a blatant disregard for the court's authority.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2234 [55]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A director can be held responsible for the acts of another person (such as an employee or agent) that constitute a breach of a court injunction if the director failed to take all reasonable steps to prevent that person from acting in breach.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2234 [68]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A party subject to an injunction has a positive duty to take all reasonable steps to prevent another person from acting in a way that would breach the injunction. Failure to do so can render the party liable for contempt.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2234 [70]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defence that a party was not personally involved in transactions that breached an injunction fails if the party is found responsible for those transactions through the actions of others whom they failed to control or prevent.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2234 [111]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The party alleging that contempt proceedings constitute an abuse of the court's process bears the burden of establishing that claim on the evidence, and the court must be satisfied that the abuse is made out.

propositions.label_en: Standard of proof for forgery in civil contempt
propositions.statement_en: An allegation of forgery made in civil contempt proceedings must be proven to the criminal standard of proof, which is beyond reasonable doubt.

propositions.label: Proceeding in absence of respondent
propositions.statement_en: A court may proceed with a hearing in the absence of a respondent who has been properly served with all relevant documents but chooses not to participate.

propositions.label: Evidential basis for setting aside statutory demand
propositions.label_en: Evidential basis for setting aside statutory demand
propositions.statement_en: A debtor seeking to set aside a statutory demand based on an alleged set-off must adduce credible evidence to substantiate the claimed debt.

propositions.label: Burden on party alleging forgery
propositions.statement_en: The party alleging that a document is a forgery bears the burden of proving that allegation, which may involve commissioning expert forensic evidence.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCFI 1518 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A constitutional challenge was raised arguing that section 54(1) of the Police Force Ordinance sets an unlawfully low threshold by requiring only that a police officer finds a citizen 'acting suspiciously' without context, rather than requiring a founded suspicion of criminal activity.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCFI 1518 [63]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A temporary restriction on personal freedom, such as being prevented from leaving a premises during a police operation, does not amount to an arrest, detention, or imprisonment. Such a restriction engages the right to personal freedom under Basic Law Article 28 but does not engage the more stringent protections against arbitrary detention under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 5.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCFI 1518 [64]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The right to personal freedom under Basic Law Article 28 is not an absolute right and does not permit a person to resist or obstruct the lawful execution of duty by public officers.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCFI 1518 [70]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A conventional ground for judicial review of police action under section 54 is that, on the objective facts, there was no basis for the requisite suspicion or reasonable belief needed to trigger the statutory powers.

propositions.label: Threshold for police stop and check under s.54(1)
propositions.label_en: Threshold for police stop and check under s.54(1)
propositions.statement_en: A constitutional challenge was raised arguing that section 54(1) of the Police Force Ordinance sets an unlawfully low threshold by requiring only that a police officer finds a citizen 'acting suspiciously' without context, rather than requiring a founded suspicion of criminal activity.

propositions.label: Distinction between restriction and deprivation of liberty
propositions.label_en: Distinction between restriction and deprivation of liberty
propositions.statement_en: A temporary restriction on personal freedom, such as being prevented from leaving a premises during a police operation, does not amount to an arrest, detention, or imprisonment. Such a restriction engages the right to personal freedom under Basic Law Article 28 but does not engage the more stringent protections against arbitrary detention under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 5.

propositions.label: Scope of Basic Law Article 28 right
propositions.label_en: Scope of Basic Law Article 28 right
propositions.statement_en: The right to personal freedom under Basic Law Article 28 is not an absolute right and does not permit a person to resist or obstruct the lawful execution of duty by public officers.

propositions.label: Judicial review ground for police powers
propositions.label_en: Judicial review ground for police powers
propositions.statement_en: A conventional ground for judicial review of police action under section 54 is that, on the objective facts, there was no basis for the requisite suspicion or reasonable belief needed to trigger the statutory powers.

edges.explanation: The present judgment cites this case as an example where a similar constitutional challenge was raised regarding police powers and the distinction between restriction and deprivation of liberty.
The present judgment notes that this case applied the principle from Wong Tze Yam (No.2) regarding the non-absolute nature of the Basic Law Article 28 right.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1286 [7]-[8], [15]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When determining the reserve price for a compulsory sale under the Land (Compulsory Sale for Redevelopment) Ordinance, the phrase 'on its own' in Schedule 2 §2(a) requires the tribunal to value the subject lot based on its standalone redevelopment potential, not on its potential value when combined with adjoining properties owned by the same applicant.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1286 [8]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If a tribunal were to consider the enhanced value from merging a lot with adjoining properties when setting a reserve price, the party asserting such additional value must provide concrete evidence to establish its probability and amount, beyond mere assertions from a valuation surveyor.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1286 [23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Principles established in prior cases must be applied only within the specific legal context and issue they decided; passages from a judgment addressing one issue (like costs) are not directly relevant to interpreting different statutory provisions (like valuation criteria).

propositions.label: Interpretation of 'on its own' in reserve price valuation
propositions.label_en: Interpretation of 'on its own' in reserve price valuation
propositions.statement_en: When determining the reserve price for a compulsory sale under the Land (Compulsory Sale for Redevelopment) Ordinance, the phrase 'on its own' in Schedule 2 §2(a) requires the tribunal to value the subject lot based on its standalone redevelopment potential, not on its potential value when combined with adjoining properties owned by the same applicant.

propositions.label: Burden of proof for merged site enhancement value
propositions.label_en: Burden of proof for merged site enhancement value
propositions.statement_en: If a tribunal were to consider the enhanced value from merging a lot with adjoining properties when setting a reserve price, the party asserting such additional value must provide concrete evidence to establish its probability and amount, beyond mere assertions from a valuation surveyor.

propositions.label: Contextual limitation of case law principles
propositions.label_en: Contextual limitation of case law principles
propositions.statement_en: Principles established in prior cases must be applied only within the specific legal context and issue they decided; passages from a judgment addressing one issue (like costs) are not directly relevant to interpreting different statutory provisions (like valuation criteria).

edges.explanation: The present judgment distinguishes the principle of equivalence from that case as not directly relevant to the construction of the specific statutory provision on reserve price valuation.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 926 [23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The quantity of drugs and the act of attempting to flee or dispose of them are not, by themselves, sufficient to prove trafficking, as they are equally consistent with simple possession. The prosecution must prove trafficking beyond reasonable doubt based on additional evidence.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 926 [21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For first-time drug offenders, imprisonment is not always the most appropriate sentence; in suitable cases, the public interest may be better served by prioritizing the offender's rehabilitation over deterrence.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 926 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where a statute provides a specific power of forfeiture for certain cases, the general forfeiture power should not be used for those cases, as the specific provision governs.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 926 [35]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Forfeiture proceedings engage constitutional protections for a fair hearing because they affect property rights. Such proceedings are civil in nature, and the applicant bears the burden of proof.

propositions.label: Possession vs trafficking distinction
propositions.statement_en: The quantity of drugs and the act of attempting to flee or dispose of them are not, by themselves, sufficient to prove trafficking, as they are equally consistent with simple possession. The prosecution must prove trafficking beyond reasonable doubt based on additional evidence.

propositions.label: Sentencing focus for first-time drug offenders
propositions.label_en: Sentencing focus for first-time drug offenders
propositions.statement_en: For first-time drug offenders, imprisonment is not always the most appropriate sentence; in suitable cases, the public interest may be better served by prioritizing the offender's rehabilitation over deterrence.

propositions.label: Forfeiture: general vs specific power
propositions.statement_en: Where a statute provides a specific power of forfeiture for certain cases, the general forfeiture power should not be used for those cases, as the specific provision governs.

propositions.label: Fair hearing in forfeiture proceedings
propositions.statement_en: Forfeiture proceedings engage constitutional protections for a fair hearing because they affect property rights. Such proceedings are civil in nature, and the applicant bears the burden of proof.

edges.explanation: The present judgment approves and applies the reasoning from Yeung Shek Kin regarding the relationship between general and specific forfeiture powers.
The present judgment approves and applies the reasoning from Yeung Shek Kin regarding the relationship between general and specific forfeiture powers.
The judgment cites Wong Hon Sun for the constitutional principle that forfeiture proceedings require a fair hearing as they affect property rights.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKDC 1022 [5]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court is not bound to accept a guilty plea to a lesser charge if the prosecution does not accept it and proceeds to trial on the more serious charge.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKDC 1022 [8]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A sentencing court may add to a sentence to account for a latent risk identified from the circumstances of the offense, such as the quantity of items involved.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKDC 1022 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Offenses of a different nature should, as a matter of principle, attract consecutive sentences.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKDC 1022 [12]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The principle of consecutive sentencing may be moderated where the facts of separate charges are connected, and one offense may be partly caused by the circumstances of another.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKDC 1022 [13]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When ordering sentences for multiple offenses to run consecutively, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive.

propositions.label: Acceptance of guilty plea
propositions.statement_en: A court is not bound to accept a guilty plea to a lesser charge if the prosecution does not accept it and proceeds to trial on the more serious charge.

propositions.label: Sentencing for latent risk
propositions.statement_en: A sentencing court may add to a sentence to account for a latent risk identified from the circumstances of the offense, such as the quantity of items involved.

propositions.label: Consecutive sentences for different offenses
propositions.statement_en: Offenses of a different nature should, as a matter of principle, attract consecutive sentences.

propositions.label: Mitigation for connection between offenses
propositions.statement_en: The principle of consecutive sentencing may be moderated where the facts of separate charges are connected, and one offense may be partly caused by the circumstances of another.

propositions.label: Application of totality principle
propositions.statement_en: When ordering sentences for multiple offenses to run consecutively, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1301 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The prosecution has a discretion to decide whether and how to make a closing speech, and this right is not limited to exceptional circumstances or required to be exercised sparingly, provided the principle of a fair trial is maintained.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1301 [3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: There should be no rigid restriction on the length or content of the prosecution's closing speech; it should be commensurate with the issues and complexity of the trial and must avoid unfair comments, with the judge having a duty to prevent unfair or irrelevant submissions.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1301 [63]-[64]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant's right to a fair trial is absolute, but a finding of unfairness and an unsafe conviction requires a persistent, prejudicial, and gross departure from proper standards that is irremediable.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1301 [39]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If an appellant abandons an appeal because they were misled by wrong legal advice into misunderstanding the consequences of pursuing it, the abandonment may be treated as a nullity in law, as it was not done knowingly.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 1301 [5], [7], [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Court of Appeal may review and set aside a sentence if it is found to be wrong in principle and/or manifestly inadequate, replacing it with an appropriate sentence.

propositions.statement_en: There should be no rigid restriction on the length or content of the prosecution's closing speech; it should be commensurate with the issues and complexity of the trial and must avoid unfair comments, with the judge having a duty to prevent unfair or irrelevant submissions.

propositions.statement_en: A defendant's right to a fair trial is absolute, but a finding of unfairness and an unsafe conviction requires a persistent, prejudicial, and gross departure from proper standards that is irremediable.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 890 [23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A judge may answer a jury's question by providing a written reply, discussed with counsel in open court beforehand, as an acceptable alternative to recalling the jury to open court, provided the principle of open and transparent justice is maintained.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 890 [50]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Even if a judge's handling of a jury question is irregular, it does not automatically constitute a material irregularity requiring the appeal to be allowed. If it were a material irregularity, the appellate court may still apply the proviso to dismiss the appeal if no substantial miscarriage of justice has occurred.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 890 [60]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for a robbery in private premises involving multiple assailants acting in concert, pre-planning, violence, and the display of weapons including firearms, the starting point must adequately reflect these serious aggravating features. Disregarding the impact of displaying a firearm is an error in principle.

paragraphs.label: [1980] HKLR 890 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A jury's question must be considered as a whole to understand what is puzzling the jury, and individual parts should not be taken out of context, especially where jurors may not be native English speakers.

propositions.label: Jury communication procedure
propositions.statement_en: A judge may answer a jury's question by providing a written reply, discussed with counsel in open court beforehand, as an acceptable alternative to recalling the jury to open court, provided the principle of open and transparent justice is maintained.

propositions.label: Material irregularity and the proviso
propositions.statement_en: Even if a judge's handling of a jury question is irregular, it does not automatically constitute a material irregularity requiring the appeal to be allowed. If it were a material irregularity, the appellate court may still apply the proviso to dismiss the appeal if no substantial miscarriage of justice has occurred.

propositions.label: Sentencing for aggravated robbery
propositions.statement_en: In sentencing for a robbery in private premises involving multiple assailants acting in concert, pre-planning, violence, and the display of weapons including firearms, the starting point must adequately reflect these serious aggravating features. Disregarding the impact of displaying a firearm is an error in principle.

propositions.label: Assessment of jury questions
propositions.statement_en: A jury's question must be considered as a whole to understand what is puzzling the jury, and individual parts should not be taken out of context, especially where jurors may not be native English speakers.

The judgment applies the principle from R v Lamb, as cited in another case, that a written reply to a jury is an acceptable alternative to an oral answer in open court.

paragraphs.label: [1981] HKC 129 [11]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant is not entitled to a full one-third sentencing discount for a guilty plea if the circumstances do not warrant it, and granting such a discount when unjustified can render a sentence wrong in principle and manifestly inadequate.

paragraphs.label: [1981] HKC 129 [62]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A judge does not have the right to require the prosecution to proceed on a more serious charge than the one they have chosen to proceed with. The converse also applies: a judge cannot require the prosecution to proceed on a less serious or alternative charge.

propositions.label: Sentencing discount for guilty plea
propositions.statement_en: A defendant is not entitled to a full one-third sentencing discount for a guilty plea if the circumstances do not warrant it, and granting such a discount when unjustified can render a sentence wrong in principle and manifestly inadequate.

propositions.label: Prosecutorial discretion on charges
propositions.statement_en: A judge does not have the right to require the prosecution to proceed on a more serious charge than the one they have chosen to proceed with. The converse also applies: a judge cannot require the prosecution to proceed on a less serious or alternative charge.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites Chow v DPP for the principle that a judge cannot require the prosecution to proceed on a more serious charge.
The judgment notes that the High Court of Australia in Maxwell v R referred with approval to the principle from Chow v DPP.
The judgment states that the Privy Council in Mohit v DPP subsequently acknowledged the principle discussed in Chow v DPP.
The judgment cites this recent case, which incidentally came from the same judge, though the specific holding is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.
The judgment cites R v Coslett for providing helpful guidance on sentencing principles applicable to the offence of perverting the course of public justice.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 508 [128]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The intention to possess required for adverse possession is an intention to possess the land in one's own name and on one's own behalf, to the exclusion of all others, including the paper title owner, as far as is reasonably practicable and legally possible.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 508 [128]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The intention to possess necessary for adverse possession is not established if the evidence points to a contrary conclusion or if the claimant's acts are ambiguous and open to more than one interpretation.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 508 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A co-owner who grants a licence to a stranger for the occupation of a common part of a building cannot acquire a right of action in respect of that stranger's occupation.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 508 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An essential feature of a tenancy in common is that, apart from the unity of possession, each co-tenant's interest is separate and distinct, and each can deal with their own share as they see fit.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 508 [125]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For possession or occupation to be considered licensed or permissive, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which consent, permission, or licence (express or implied) from the co-owner(s) or principal tenant(s) can be inferred.

propositions.label: Adverse possession intention formulation
propositions.statement_en: The intention to possess required for adverse possession is an intention to possess the land in one's own name and on one's own behalf, to the exclusion of all others, including the paper title owner, as far as is reasonably practicable and legally possible.

propositions.label: Adverse possession - equivocal acts
propositions.statement_en: The intention to possess necessary for adverse possession is not established if the evidence points to a contrary conclusion or if the claimant's acts are ambiguous and open to more than one interpretation.

propositions.label: Co-owner's licence to stranger
propositions.statement_en: A co-owner who grants a licence to a stranger for the occupation of a common part of a building cannot acquire a right of action in respect of that stranger's occupation.

propositions.label: Tenancy in common essential feature
propositions.statement_en: An essential feature of a tenancy in common is that, apart from the unity of possession, each co-tenant's interest is separate and distinct, and each can deal with their own share as they see fit.

propositions.label: Burden of proof for licence/consent
propositions.statement_en: For possession or occupation to be considered licensed or permissive, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which consent, permission, or licence (express or implied) from the co-owner(s) or principal tenant(s) can be inferred.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites this case for the legal principles concerning the nature of a tenancy in common and the rights of a co-owner who grants a licence to a stranger.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKLRD 499 [128]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The intention to possess required for adverse possession is established when a person acts in their own name and on their own behalf to exclude the world at large, including the paper title owner, as far as is reasonably practicable and allowed by law. This intention is not established if evidence points to a contrary conclusion or if the acts are equivocal and capable of multiple interpretations.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKLRD 499 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If a co-owner grants a license to a stranger for the occupation of a common part of a building, no right of action accrues to that co-owner in respect of the stranger's occupation.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKLRD 499 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An essential feature of a tenancy in common is that, apart from the unity of possession, each co-tenant's interest is separate and distinct, and each can deal with their own share as they see fit.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKLRD 499 [125]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: To establish that possession or occupation was pursuant to a license or consent of a co-owner or principal tenant, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which such an inference can be drawn. Absent such evidence, no license or consent is found.

propositions.label: Adverse possession intention formulation

propositions.label: Tenancy in common essential feature

propositions.label: Burden for proving license/consent
propositions.statement_en: To establish that possession or occupation was pursuant to a license or consent of a co-owner or principal tenant, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which such an inference can be drawn. Absent such evidence, no license or consent is found.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites this case for the legal principles concerning the essential features of a tenancy in common and the rights of a co-owner who grants a license to a stranger.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKCA 194 [128]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The intention to possess required for adverse possession is defined as the intention to possess the land in one's own name and on one's own behalf, to exclude the world at large, including the paper title owner, so far as is reasonably practicable and as the law allows. This intention is not established if evidence points to a contrary conclusion or if the claimant's acts are equivocal and capable of more than one interpretation.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKCA 194 [43]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A key feature of a tenancy in common is that, apart from unity of possession, each co-owner's interest is separate and distinct, allowing each to deal with their own share as they see fit. Consequently, if a co-owner grants a licence to a stranger for the occupation of a common part of a building, no right of action accrues to that co-owner in respect of the stranger's occupation.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKCA 194 [125]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For a claim that possession or occupation of property was pursuant to the consent, permission, or licence of a co-owner or principal tenant, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which such an inference can be drawn. Absent such evidence, the claim of licensed possession fails.

paragraphs.label: [2005] HKCA 194 [87]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Inconsistencies between a witness's oral and written evidence do not necessarily undermine the overall reliability of their evidence and recollection of events, particularly where the witness candidly admits the limitations of their memory and the sources of their knowledge.

propositions.label: Adverse possession intention formulation
propositions.statement_en: The intention to possess required for adverse possession is defined as the intention to possess the land in one's own name and on one's own behalf, to exclude the world at large, including the paper title owner, so far as is reasonably practicable and as the law allows. This intention is not established if evidence points to a contrary conclusion or if the claimant's acts are equivocal and capable of more than one interpretation.

propositions.label: Tenancy in common and rights over common parts
propositions.label_en: Tenancy in common and rights over common parts
propositions.statement_en: A key feature of a tenancy in common is that, apart from unity of possession, each co-owner's interest is separate and distinct, allowing each to deal with their own share as they see fit. Consequently, if a co-owner grants a licence to a stranger for the occupation of a common part of a building, no right of action accrues to that co-owner in respect of the stranger's occupation.

propositions.label: Burden of proof for licence/consent in possession claims
propositions.label_en: Burden of proof for licence/consent in possession claims
propositions.statement_en: For a claim that possession or occupation of property was pursuant to the consent, permission, or licence of a co-owner or principal tenant, there must be sufficient evidence of an overt act or circumstances from which such an inference can be drawn. Absent such evidence, the claim of licensed possession fails.

propositions.label: Assessment of witness reliability despite inconsistencies
propositions.label_en: Assessment of witness reliability despite inconsistencies
propositions.statement_en: Inconsistencies between a witness's oral and written evidence do not necessarily undermine the overall reliability of their evidence and recollection of events, particularly where the witness candidly admits the limitations of their memory and the sources of their knowledge.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites this case for the legal principles concerning the nature of a tenancy in common and the rights of co-owners regarding common parts.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 479 [10.3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The legal maxim ex turpi causa non oritur actio (no action arises from a base cause) does not apply to claims for adverse possession, regardless of whether the reliance or proportionality approach is taken.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 479 [10.4]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A squatter's occupation of land in breach of a government lease's user covenant or building ordinances does not, in itself, prevent a successful claim for adverse possession.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 479 [23.5]-[23.6]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The ex turpi causa maxim should be applied as an instrument of public policy and is generally confined to criminal, quasi-criminal acts, or civil wrongs involving dishonesty that engage the public interest, not to breaches of contract or statutory duties affecting private interests.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 479 [15.6]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A successive squatter can add a preceding squatter's period of adverse possession to their own to compute the requisite time, provided the possession is continuous, and this principle applies to squatters within the same family.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKCA 479 [15.5]-[15.6]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may find adverse possession based on a period shorter than that pleaded, provided the squatter's case is clearly one of continuous possession and the paper title holder had a fair opportunity to challenge the evidence.

propositions.label_en: Illegality of occupation and adverse possession
propositions.statement_en: A squatter's occupation of land in breach of a government lease's user covenant or building ordinances does not, in itself, prevent a successful claim for adverse possession.

propositions.label_en: Scope of ex turpi causa maxim

propositions.label: Tacking possession periods
propositions.statement_en: A successive squatter can add a preceding squatter's period of adverse possession to their own to compute the requisite time, provided the possession is continuous, and this principle applies to squatters within the same family.

propositions.label: Adverse possession based on shorter period
propositions.label_en: Adverse possession based on shorter period
propositions.statement_en: A court may find adverse possession based on a period shorter than that pleaded, provided the squatter's case is clearly one of continuous possession and the paper title holder had a fair opportunity to challenge the evidence.

paragraphs.label: [2017] AC 467 [35]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A claimant seeking to establish an interest in property can recover if they are not forced to plead or rely on illegality to prove their claim.

paragraphs.label: [2017] AC 467 [57]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Not every breach of foreign law will prevent enforcement of a contract; only sufficiently serious breaches that reflect important policies of the foreign state may render enforcement contrary to public policy.

paragraphs.label: [2017] AC 467 [55]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court should not apply a mechanistic rule to illegality defenses but should consider the underlying purpose of the prohibition, other relevant public policies, and the possibility of disproportionate consequences.

paragraphs.label: [1940] AC 880 [1]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Whether an act constitutes a nuisance depends on an assessment of all the specific circumstances of the case, not just an abstract consideration of the act itself.

paragraphs.label: [1940] AC 880 [17]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When a primary claim in nuisance fails, a dependent claim in negligence that relies on the same factual foundation may also fail as a consequence.

paragraphs.label: [1940] AC 880 [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An alleged breach of duty by a building manager under a Deed of Mutual Covenant does not require separate consideration if the primary ruling that no nuisance existed is upheld.

propositions.label: Nuisance determination factors

propositions.label: Manager's duty breach consideration

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCA 192 [18]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A judge has a duty to direct the jury on an obvious alternative verdict that is supported by the evidence. The way the defence case is presented is relevant in determining whether such an alternative arises; if the factual basis for the alternative does not emerge from the defence case, this will usually be decisive against the need for a direction.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCA 192 [20]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where a defendant raises the defence of duress, the judge must direct the jury that if they find the defendant's account of duress to be true or possibly true, they must acquit the defendant.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCA 192 [35]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The prosecution may prove the agreement required for a conspiracy by asking the jury to infer its existence from the totality of the evidence presented, such as CCTV footage showing joint activities.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCA 192 [42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: There is no basis for a judge to direct the jury on an alternative verdict (e.g., under a specific statutory provision) when the defendant's sole defence is that they committed the act but under duress.

propositions.label: Duty to direct on alternative verdicts

propositions.statement_en: Where a defendant raises the defence of duress, the judge must direct the jury that if they find the defendant's account of duress to be true or possibly true, they must acquit the defendant.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2283 [18]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where there is an obvious alternative verdict supported by the evidence, the trial judge has a duty to direct the jury about that alternative option. The way the defence case is presented is relevant in determining whether sufficient evidence exists for such an alternative verdict.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2283 [20]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If a defendant raises the defence of duress and the jury finds the defendant's account to be true or possibly true, they must acquit the defendant. The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond reasonable doubt, including disproving the defence of duress.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2283 [42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: There is no basis for a judge to direct the jury on an alternative verdict for a lesser offence under section 37 when the defendant's sole defence is that they committed the act but under duress. The jury's rejection of the duress defence resolves the matter.

paragraphs.label: [2022] HKCFI 2283 [text block after para 34]

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 946 [15]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: If the court finds it is reasonably possible that the defendant's testimony denying the offence is true, the defendant must be acquitted, even if the court does not find the defendant to be a completely truthful witness overall.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 946 [27]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where one charge is factually dependent on the events of another charge, and the prosecution case on the foundational charge is not accepted, the court cannot accept the prosecution case on the dependent charge either.

propositions.label: Standard of proof for acquittal
propositions.statement_en: If the court finds it is reasonably possible that the defendant's testimony denying the offence is true, the defendant must be acquitted, even if the court does not find the defendant to be a completely truthful witness overall.

propositions.label: Logical dependency of charges
propositions.statement_en: Where one charge is factually dependent on the events of another charge, and the prosecution case on the foundational charge is not accepted, the court cannot accept the prosecution case on the dependent charge either.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 1313 [1]-[6]

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKDC 1313 [5]-[6]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The lawful importation of a species listed in Appendix II of the Ordinance (such as American ginseng) is conditional upon the specimen being accompanied by a valid CITES certificate or a certificate in lieu issued under the Ordinance.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: Sentences for offenses arising from the same incident or closely connected acts should generally be served concurrently, while sentences for distinct offenses posing separate risks may be ordered to run consecutively.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFA 7 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offence under s.30(1)(b), the prosecution must prove the defendant knew or suspected that the investigation concerned was specifically for an offence under Part II of the relevant ordinance.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFA 7 [70]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The proper construction of s.30(1)(b) requires the disclosure to convey, in substance, that the individual is subject to an investigation in respect of an offence alleged or suspected under Part II.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFA 7 [72]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: While statutory provisions are to be given a purposive interpretation, the court cannot attribute a meaning to a provision that the statutory language, understood in context and purpose, is incapable of bearing.

propositions.statement_en: While statutory provisions are to be given a purposive interpretation, the court cannot attribute a meaning to a provision that the statutory language, understood in context and purpose, is incapable of bearing.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 422 [8]-[9]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Section 30(1) of the ICAC Ordinance is intended to protect investigations, specifically by prohibiting disclosures that might alert a subject to an investigation and thereby risk evidence being destroyed or the investigation being prejudiced.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 422 [38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The proper construction of section 30(1)(b) of the ICAC Ordinance requires an examination of its legislative history to ascertain its intended scope and application.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 422 [55]-[56]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The legislative history indicates that section 30(1) was intended to be narrowed in application to cover only investigations into offences under Part II of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance, specifically corruption offences, rather than all ICAC investigations.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 422 [56]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A proposed amendment to section 30(1) considered during its legislative review was to restrict its application to persons who knew or suspected that an investigation was taking place, indicating the potential importance of a knowledge element.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFI 422 [83]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For a defendant to rely on the 'reasonable excuse' defence under section 30, they must provide sufficient evidence to support it, and the belief forming the basis of the excuse must itself be reasonable.

propositions.label: Scope of s.30(1) ICAC Ordinance
propositions.statement_en: Section 30(1) of the ICAC Ordinance is intended to protect investigations, specifically by prohibiting disclosures that might alert a subject to an investigation and thereby risk evidence being destroyed or the investigation being prejudiced.

propositions.label: Legislative intent of s.30(1)(b)
propositions.statement_en: The proper construction of section 30(1)(b) of the ICAC Ordinance requires an examination of its legislative history to ascertain its intended scope and application.

propositions.label: Application to Part II offences
propositions.statement_en: The legislative history indicates that section 30(1) was intended to be narrowed in application to cover only investigations into offences under Part II of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance, specifically corruption offences, rather than all ICAC investigations.

propositions.label: Knowledge element for offence
propositions.statement_en: A proposed amendment to section 30(1) considered during its legislative review was to restrict its application to persons who knew or suspected that an investigation was taking place, indicating the potential importance of a knowledge element.

propositions.label: Burden for 'reasonable excuse' defence
propositions.statement_en: For a defendant to rely on the 'reasonable excuse' defence under section 30, they must provide sufficient evidence to support it, and the belief forming the basis of the excuse must itself be reasonable.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites Ming Pao to reference the Court of Appeal's opinion on the structure and validity of the earlier version of section 30(1).

paragraphs.label: [1987] HKLR 145 [40]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For the offence of unauthorized disclosure under section 30(1)(b) of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant knew or suspected that the investigation in question was specifically in respect of an offence alleged or suspected under Part II of the Ordinance.

paragraphs.label: [1987] HKLR 145 [70]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A disclosure violates section 30(1)(b) if it conveys the substance that a person is under investigation for a Part II offence, even if the precise legal label 'Part II' is not used.

paragraphs.label: [1987] HKLR 145 [72]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: While statutes should be interpreted purposively, a court cannot assign a meaning to a statutory provision that the statutory language, understood in its context and purpose, is incapable of bearing.

propositions.label: Mens rea for s.30(1)(b) disclosure offence
propositions.label_en: Mens rea for s.30(1)(b) disclosure offence
propositions.statement_en: For the offence of unauthorized disclosure under section 30(1)(b) of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant knew or suspected that the investigation in question was specifically in respect of an offence alleged or suspected under Part II of the Ordinance.

propositions.label: Substantive disclosure requirement
propositions.statement_en: A disclosure violates section 30(1)(b) if it conveys the substance that a person is under investigation for a Part II offence, even if the precise legal label 'Part II' is not used.

propositions.label: Limits of purposive interpretation
propositions.statement_en: While statutes should be interpreted purposively, a court cannot assign a meaning to a statutory provision that the statutory language, understood in its context and purpose, is incapable of bearing.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites this case for the primary purpose of the unauthorized disclosure offence under the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance.
The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the limits of purposive statutory interpretation.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 516 [para 14]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For a charge of rape, if the defendant held a genuine but mistaken belief that the complainant was capable of giving consent (for example, believing she was of legal age), and the prosecution fails to disprove this belief, the defendant must be acquitted.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 516 [para 21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Unlike for the offence of indecent assault, there is no statutory provision stating that a person under the age of 16 cannot in law give consent for the purpose of the offence of rape.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 516 [para 21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The test for determining whether an indictment for a major offence includes an allegation of a lesser offence is whether proving the commission of the lesser offence is a necessary step or an essential ingredient in establishing the major offence.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 516 [para 34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may only convict an accused of an alternative offence if the allegations in the indictment, whether express or implied, amount to or include an allegation of that other offence, ensuring the accused is not tried for an offence not fairly disclosed.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 516 [para 42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The statutory alternatives to rape listed in Schedule 1 of the Crimes Ordinance are specific offences like procurement by threats or false pretences, and do not include offences such as unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 16 or indecent assault.

propositions.label: Mistaken belief defense for rape
propositions.statement_en: For a charge of rape, if the defendant held a genuine but mistaken belief that the complainant was capable of giving consent (for example, believing she was of legal age), and the prosecution fails to disprove this belief, the defendant must be acquitted.

propositions.label: Consent of minor under 16 for rape
propositions.label_en: Consent of minor under 16 for rape
propositions.statement_en: Unlike for the offence of indecent assault, there is no statutory provision stating that a person under the age of 16 cannot in law give consent for the purpose of the offence of rape.

propositions.label: Test for lesser included offence
propositions.statement_en: The test for determining whether an indictment for a major offence includes an allegation of a lesser offence is whether proving the commission of the lesser offence is a necessary step or an essential ingredient in establishing the major offence.

propositions.label: Condition for alternative conviction
propositions.statement_en: A court may only convict an accused of an alternative offence if the allegations in the indictment, whether express or implied, amount to or include an allegation of that other offence, ensuring the accused is not tried for an offence not fairly disclosed.

propositions.label: Statutory alternatives to rape
propositions.statement_en: The statutory alternatives to rape listed in Schedule 1 of the Crimes Ordinance are specific offences like procurement by threats or false pretences, and do not include offences such as unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 16 or indecent assault.

edges.explanation: The present judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the legal capacity of a minor under 16 to consent to rape.
The present judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the legal capacity of a minor under 16 to consent to rape.

paragraphs.label: [1973] QB 565 [para 14]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant is not guilty of rape if he held a genuine but mistaken belief that the complainant was capable of giving consent, such as believing she was of legal age. The prosecution bears the burden of proving the absence of such a belief.

paragraphs.label: [1973] QB 565 [para 21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A person under the age of 16 cannot in law give valid consent that would prevent an act from constituting the offence of rape.

paragraphs.label: [1973] QB 565 [para 21]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The test for whether an indictment includes an allegation of a lesser offence is whether proving the commission of the lesser offence is a necessary step towards establishing the major offence; that is, whether the lesser offence is an essential ingredient of the major one.

paragraphs.label: [1973] QB 565 [para 34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may only convict for an alternative offence if the allegations in the indictment amount to or include, expressly or by implication, an allegation of that other offence. This safeguards the accused's right to a fair trial by not having to address an offence not open on the indictment.

paragraphs.label: [1973] QB 565 [para 42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The statutory alternative offences for rape, as listed in Schedule 1 of the Crimes Ordinance, are procurement by threats, procurement by false pretences, and administering drugs to facilitate an unlawful sexual act. Unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 16 and indecent assault are not listed as statutory alternatives.

propositions.label: Mistaken belief defense for rape
propositions.statement_en: A defendant is not guilty of rape if he held a genuine but mistaken belief that the complainant was capable of giving consent, such as believing she was of legal age. The prosecution bears the burden of proving the absence of such a belief.

propositions.label: No valid consent from underage victim for rape
propositions.label_en: No valid consent from underage victim for rape
propositions.statement_en: A person under the age of 16 cannot in law give valid consent that would prevent an act from constituting the offence of rape.

propositions.label: Test for lesser included offence in indictment
propositions.label_en: Test for lesser included offence in indictment
propositions.statement_en: The test for whether an indictment includes an allegation of a lesser offence is whether proving the commission of the lesser offence is a necessary step towards establishing the major offence; that is, whether the lesser offence is an essential ingredient of the major one.

propositions.label: Condition for alternative conviction
propositions.statement_en: A court may only convict for an alternative offence if the allegations in the indictment amount to or include, expressly or by implication, an allegation of that other offence. This safeguards the accused's right to a fair trial by not having to address an offence not open on the indictment.

propositions.label: Statutory alternatives for rape
propositions.statement_en: The statutory alternative offences for rape, as listed in Schedule 1 of the Crimes Ordinance, are procurement by threats, procurement by false pretences, and administering drugs to facilitate an unlawful sexual act. Unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 16 and indecent assault are not listed as statutory alternatives.

edges.explanation: The present judgment notes that the principle from Harling was applied in the subsequent case of HKSAR v YHY.
The present judgment notes that the principle from Harling was applied in the subsequent case of HKSAR v YHY.
The judgment cites HKSAR v YHY as a case where the principle from Harling was applied regarding the inability of an under-16 victim to give legal consent to rape.

paragraphs.label: [1984] AC 242 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When a defendant is tried on indictment, if the jury finds them not guilty of the specific charge, but the allegations in the indictment expressly or impliedly include another offence within the court's jurisdiction, the jury may convict the defendant of that other offence.

paragraphs.label: [1984] AC 242 [15]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant may offer to plead guilty to a lesser offence than the one charged, but the prosecution is not obliged to accept that plea. If the prosecution does not accept it, the plea is a nullity or deemed withdrawn, and the trial must proceed on the original charge.

paragraphs.label: [1984] AC 242 [15]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The jury has no duty to return a verdict on a lesser offence to which a defendant has offered to plead guilty if the prosecution has not accepted that plea, as that plea is not properly before the jury.

propositions.label: Jury's power to convict on lesser included offence
propositions.label_en: Jury's power to convict on lesser included offence
propositions.statement_en: When a defendant is tried on indictment, if the jury finds them not guilty of the specific charge, but the allegations in the indictment expressly or impliedly include another offence within the court's jurisdiction, the jury may convict the defendant of that other offence.

propositions.label: Prosecution discretion on accepting lesser plea
propositions.label_en: Prosecution discretion on accepting lesser plea
propositions.statement_en: A defendant may offer to plead guilty to a lesser offence than the one charged, but the prosecution is not obliged to accept that plea. If the prosecution does not accept it, the plea is a nullity or deemed withdrawn, and the trial must proceed on the original charge.

propositions.label: Jury's role regarding unaccepted lesser pleas
propositions.label_en: Jury's role regarding unaccepted lesser pleas
propositions.statement_en: The jury has no duty to return a verdict on a lesser offence to which a defendant has offered to plead guilty if the prosecution has not accepted that plea, as that plea is not properly before the jury.

edges.explanation: The judgment cites Lillis for the test regarding when a jury may convict on a lesser included offence.
The judgment considers Maxwell in the context of a robbery charge where the defendant's case was based on procuring a different offence.
The judgment references Coutts, which dealt with a trial judge's duty to leave alternative verdicts of lesser included offences to the jury.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKDC 992 [13]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Where a person is found to be a victim of fraud, equity will impose a trust on the fraudulent recipient of the funds.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKDC 992 [13]

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1823 [a]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A trust cannot exist unless there is clearly defined property that constitutes the trust assets. A person cannot be considered a trustee of property that cannot be specifically identified.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1823 [54]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The requirement that unjust enrichment occurs 'at the expense of' the claimant can be satisfied even when there is no direct transaction between the claimant and defendant.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1823 [c]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Liability for unjust enrichment arises immediately when the defendant receives the enrichment. Subsequent dissipation of the received funds does not affect this liability.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 1823 [66]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may draw an adverse inference against a party who facilitated the perpetration of a fraud, even in the absence of direct evidence implicating them in the fraudulent scheme.

propositions.label: Identifiable property requirement for trusts

propositions.label: Adverse inference in fraud cases
propositions.statement_en: A court may draw an adverse inference against a party who facilitated the perpetration of a fraud, even in the absence of direct evidence implicating them in the fraudulent scheme.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFI 3265 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for an offense, the court should apply the established sentencing tariff unless there is a specific reason to depart from it.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFI 3265 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The defendant's role in the offense is a key factor in determining sentence, with lower-level roles generally attracting lower sentences, but the court must assess where the defendant sits within the hierarchy.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFI 3265 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A sentence may be enhanced above the starting point if specific aggravating features of the offense are present.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCFI 3265 [2]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The appropriate sentence after a trial should be determined first, before considering any discount for a guilty plea.

propositions.label: Sentencing tariff application

propositions.label: Role-based sentencing assessment

propositions.label: Aggravating features enhancement
propositions.statement_en: A sentence may be enhanced above the starting point if specific aggravating features of the offense are present.

propositions.label: Notional post-trial sentence
propositions.statement_en: The appropriate sentence after a trial should be determined first, before considering any discount for a guilty plea.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 674 [1]-[2], [5]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The Secretary for Justice must apply to appeal by way of case stated against an acquittal within 7 clear days after the reasons for verdict are recorded or after the order of acquittal, unless an extension is granted by a High Court judge.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 674 [2], [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An appeal by way of case stated against an acquittal under section 84 of the District Court Ordinance can only be made on a question of law.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: For driving to be considered dangerous, it must fall far below the standard expected of a competent and careful driver; mere carelessness or a momentary lapse of concentration does not meet this threshold.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant must be acquitted if the prosecution fails to prove all elements of the charge beyond reasonable doubt.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may find a defendant not guilty of a more serious charge but guilty of a lesser included offence if the evidence supports the lesser charge beyond reasonable doubt.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 826 [1]-[3]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant is not guilty of rape if he genuinely believed the complainant was consenting to sexual intercourse, even if that belief was mistaken, unreasonable, or based on a misunderstanding. The belief must be genuinely held.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 826 [4]-[5]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A complainant cannot consent to sexual intercourse if they are unconscious or so intoxicated that they are incapable of making a choice. However, consent given while intoxicated, even if it would not have been given when sober, is still valid consent if the person retained the capacity to make a choice.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 826 [7]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The prosecution bears the burden of proving beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant did not genuinely believe the complainant was consenting. It is not for the defendant to prove he held such a belief.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 826 [8], [i]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When deciding whether a defendant genuinely held a belief in consent, the jury should consider the presence or absence of reasonable grounds for that belief, alongside all surrounding circumstances. Reasonable grounds make it more likely the belief was genuinely held, but their absence does not mandate rejection of the defence.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 826 [9]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A genuine belief in consensual sex requires some appreciation of the other party's state of mind. The jury must consider what the defendant could have appreciated about the complainant that led him to hold that belief.

propositions.label: Mistaken belief in consent defence
propositions.statement_en: A defendant is not guilty of rape if he genuinely believed the complainant was consenting to sexual intercourse, even if that belief was mistaken, unreasonable, or based on a misunderstanding. The belief must be genuinely held.

propositions.label: Capacity to consent and intoxication
propositions.statement_en: A complainant cannot consent to sexual intercourse if they are unconscious or so intoxicated that they are incapable of making a choice. However, consent given while intoxicated, even if it would not have been given when sober, is still valid consent if the person retained the capacity to make a choice.

paragraphs.label: [1976] AC 182 [para 22]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant cannot be guilty of rape if he genuinely believed the complainant was consenting, even if that belief was mistaken. The prosecution bears the burden of proving the defendant did not hold such a belief.

paragraphs.label: [1976] AC 182 [para 22]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A judicial direction on honest belief in consent is not required in every rape case. It is only necessary where the evidence leaves room for the possibility that the defendant held such a belief.

paragraphs.label: [1976] AC 182 [para 32]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: For a rape conviction, the prosecution must prove the defendant either knew the complainant was not consenting or was reckless as to whether she was consenting.

paragraphs.label: [1976] AC 182 [para 32]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A defendant is reckless as to consent if he realized there was a risk the complainant was not consenting and proceeded with sexual intercourse anyway.

paragraphs.label: [1976] AC 182 [] (paragraph beginning 'As to whether he knew...')
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The prosecution can prove a defendant's knowledge of non-consent through inference from the proven facts, without requiring a direct admission from the defendant.

propositions.label: Honest belief in consent

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 573 [para 36]-[para 42]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A jury direction regarding an honest but mistaken belief in consent is only required when the evidence provides a proper foundation for the possibility that the defendant genuinely held such a belief. The judge should not give unnecessary or irrelevant directions.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 573 [para 48]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The defence of a genuine but mistaken belief in consent must be put before the jury if there is a proper evidential basis for it, regardless of whether the evidence comes from the prosecution or the defence case.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 573 [para 59]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The mens rea for rape requires proof that the defendant, at the time of intercourse, knew the complainant did not consent or was reckless as to whether she consented. Recklessness involves foreseeing the risk of non-consent and proceeding regardless.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 573 [para 60]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: It is a complete defence to rape if the defendant genuinely believed the complainant was consenting, even if that belief was mistaken or unreasonable. The prosecution bears the burden of disproving this belief beyond reasonable doubt.

paragraphs.label: [2025] HKCA 573 [para 75]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Evidence that the defendant held a genuine belief in consent can come from the defendant's own testimony or from the prosecution's case. If such evidence is raised, the jury must be directed to consider whether the prosecution has disproved it.

propositions.label: Defence of genuine mistaken belief
propositions.statement_en: It is a complete defence to rape if the defendant genuinely believed the complainant was consenting, even if that belief was mistaken or unreasonable. The prosecution bears the burden of disproving this belief beyond reasonable doubt.

propositions.label: Source of evidence for belief
